files.response: This module provides ANSI escape sequence parsing for the terminal UI (TUI), converting ANSI-formatted text into ratatui-compatible text objects. It handles two main use cases: single-line content via `ansi_escape_line()` and multi-line content via `ansi_escape()`, while also normalizing tabs to spaces to prevent visual misalignment issues in transcript rendering where line numbers or other prefixes might interact poorly with tab characters.

A command-line utility that generates TypeScript bindings and JSON schemas for the Codex app-server protocol, with optional code formatting via Prettier. It serves as the entry point for the code generation pipeline, delegating the actual generation logic to the `codex_app_server_protocol` library.

This is a utility binary that regenerates TypeScript and JSON schema fixture files from source definitions. It accepts optional arguments to specify the schema root directory, a Prettier executable for formatting output, and a flag to include experimental API methods. The tool serves as a build-time helper to keep vendored schema fixtures in sync with their canonical definitions.

This file defines the infrastructure for marking and tracking experimental features in the protocol. It provides a trait (`ExperimentalApi`) that allows protocol types to declare whether they're experimental and return a reason identifier, along with a registry system to collect all experimental fields across the codebase and generate consistent error messages when experimental capabilities are required.

This file generates TypeScript type definitions and JSON schemas from the Codex app-server protocol, with built-in support for filtering out experimental/unstable APIs. It takes protocol structures (like `ClientRequest`, `ServerNotification`) and converts them into consumer-friendly TypeScript files and a bundled JSON schema, optionally removing experimental features marked for future removal. The code handles both code generation (via `ts-rs` and `schemars`) and post-processing (filtering experimental types, running Prettier, managing namespaces, pruning unused imports) to produce clean, organized output that consumers can import from.

This file defines the data structures for a simplified JSON-RPC protocol used in the app-server communication layer. It provides serializable types for requests, notifications, responses, and error messages that can be sent over the wire, while deliberately omitting the standard "jsonrpc": "2.0" version field that true JSON-RPC 2.0 requires. The structs are annotated to generate JSON schemas and TypeScript type definitions automatically, ensuring type safety across both backend and frontend.

This library module serves as the public interface for the app-server protocol, aggregating and re-exporting types and functions from internal submodules covering JSON-RPC communication, protocol definitions (v1 and v2), experimental APIs, and schema/fixture utilities. It acts as a facade that simplifies downstream consumers' imports by centralizing the protocol's public API in one place.

# Summary

This file defines the JSON-RPC protocol schema for bidirectional communication between a Codex client and server. It uses declarative macros to generate enums for `ClientRequest`, `ServerRequest`, `ServerNotification`, and `ClientNotification`, along with their associated serialization, TypeScript export, and JSON schema generation functions. The file establishes both legacy v1 API endpoints (conversation-based) and newer v2 API endpoints (thread and turn-based), including support for experimental feature gating and approval workflows for commands and file changes.

This file provides protocol version migration by implementing a converter that transforms command execution parameters from the v1 API schema to the v2 schema, handling field mappings and type conversions (such as normalizing timeout values) between the two protocol versions.

This file serves as the module namespace organizer for the app-server protocol layer, declaring and exposing the main protocol submodules (common, thread_history, v1, v2) and internal mappers. It acts as the public entrypoint that allows consuming code to access protocol definitions and utilities through a clean, organized interface.

This file reconstructs a conversation's turn history from a sequence of persisted events and rollout items. It parses low-level protocol events (user messages, agent responses, reasoning steps, etc.) into a higher-level `Turn` structure, handling edge cases like thread rollbacks, interrupted turns, and compacted history markers. The builder maintains state across events to group related items into logical conversation turns, with special logic to preserve turn boundaries and manage backward compatibility with older event streams.

This file defines the request and response message types for the Codex app server protocol (v1). It establishes the data contracts for all client-server interactions, including conversation management (create, resume, fork, list), authentication flows (login via API key or ChatGPT), user configuration, command execution approvals, and message sending. The structs are serializable/deserializable with JSON schema generation and TypeScript type bindings, enabling both backend validation and frontend type safety across the protocol boundary.

# Summary

This file defines the **API v2 protocol types** for the Codex application server, serving as the primary serialization/deserialization layer between clients and the backend. It translates internal core types (which use snake_case/kebab-case naming) into camelCase JSON-compatible structures and generates TypeScript type definitions for web clients. The file encompasses configuration management, authentication, thread/turn lifecycle, user input, item streaming, approvals, and rate limiting—essentially the complete request/response and notification schema for the app-server protocol.

This file manages the generation and normalization of schema fixture files (TypeScript and JSON schemas) used for testing and reference. It provides functions to regenerate schema outputs in dedicated directories and read them back, while canonicalizing JSON and normalizing line endings to ensure consistent, platform-independent fixture comparisons. The canonicalization logic intelligently sorts certain JSON arrays and object keys only when safe to do so, preserving semantic equivalence while avoiding false test failures across different operating systems.

This test file verifies that vendored schema fixture files remain synchronized with freshly generated schema output. It generates a new set of schema fixtures in a temporary directory, compares them against the committed fixtures, and fails with a detailed diff if any files or contents diverge—helping developers catch when schema changes need to be re-exported.

This is a test client for the Codex app-server that spawns the server as a subprocess and exercises its JSON-RPC API through various scenarios. It provides a CLI with multiple commands for testing different capabilities—sending messages, resuming threads, triggering approval flows, testing authentication, and querying models and rate limits. The client handles bidirectional JSON-RPC communication, manages server notifications and approvals (automatically approving command execution and file change requests), and streams the server's responses back to the user for inspection.

This is a thin entry point that delegates to the actual test client implementation. It exists to provide a runnable binary executable that initializes and runs the application server test client logic defined in the library crate.

# Summary

This file handles the conversion and emission of internal core events to external protocol notifications and responses. It acts as a bridge between the `CodexThread` event stream and the API clients, translating low-level events (like file patches, command executions, and collaborative agent interactions) into versioned protocol messages. The file manages both API v1 and v2 compatibility, tracks turn state, and coordinates approval workflows by sending user requests and processing responses back to the core.

# Summary

This file implements `CodexMessageProcessor`, the main JSON-RPC request handler for the Codex application server. It processes client requests for thread (conversation) management, authentication, model selection, skills, MCP servers, and various AI turn operations. The processor bridges the protocol layer with core execution engines by routing requests to thread managers, config builders, and backend clients, while managing listener subscriptions that stream events back to connected clients.

This file exposes a JSON-RPC API for reading and writing configuration values in the Codex app server. It wraps the core `ConfigService` to handle client requests for configuration reads, writes, and requirements, while translating between internal core types and the protocol types used by API consumers. It also maps errors from the core service into standardized JSON-RPC error responses.

This file handles the response callback for dynamic tool calls initiated by the AI. When a tool execution completes (or fails), it receives the result through a channel, deserializes it, and submits the response back to the conversation thread for further processing. It includes error handling at multiple stages—channel failure, deserialization errors, and submission failures—ensuring graceful degradation with fallback error messages.

Defines standardized error codes for API responses, including invalid request, internal server error, and service overload conditions. These constants appear to follow JSON-RPC error code conventions and are used throughout the application to communicate specific failure states to clients.

This file provides filtering logic for thread sources in the app server, enabling callers to request threads from specific origins (CLI, VSCode, sub-agents, etc.). The `compute_source_filters` function converts user-specified source filters into a format suitable for database queries, splitting sources into those that can be filtered at query time versus those requiring post-processing checks. The `source_kind_matches` helper then applies these filters to determine whether a given thread source matches the requested criteria.

This module performs fuzzy file searching across specified directory roots, returning ranked results with match scores and character indices. It wraps a lower-level file search implementation, configuring it to run in a bounded thread pool (up to 12 threads), respecting cancellation signals, and sorting results by relevance score before returning them to the caller.

This file is the main application server entry point for Codex. It orchestrates the core runtime by initializing configuration, setting up telemetry and logging, and coordinating three async tasks: a transport layer (handling stdio or WebSocket connections), a message processor (dispatching JSON-RPC requests), and an outbound router (writing responses back to clients). The `OutboundControlEvent` enum and surrounding coordination logic decouple the processor and router tasks to avoid contention when handling potentially slow network writes.

This is the entry point for the Codex application server. It parses command-line arguments to determine the transport endpoint (defaulting to stdio, with optional WebSocket support), loads configuration with debug-friendly overrides for testing, and delegates to the core server runtime. The file exists to bootstrap the server with the correct transport mechanism and configuration paths, particularly supporting integration tests that need to use temporary config files.

This file implements the main JSON-RPC message dispatcher for the Codex app server, routing incoming client requests to appropriate handlers. It manages connection initialization state, enforces capability-based access control (e.g., experimental API gating), handles configuration read/write operations directly, and delegates other requests to a specialized message processor. It also bridges external authentication refresh requests back to the client through an async callback mechanism.

This file exposes the available AI models to the application server by querying the thread manager's model registry, filtering for user-facing options, and transforming internal model presets into the API response format. It serves as the bridge between the core model management system and the protocol layer that clients consume.

This file defines the messaging infrastructure for sending responses, notifications, and requests from the server to connected clients over JSON-RPC. It manages outgoing message routing (targeted to specific connections or broadcast), generates unique request IDs, and maintains a callback registry to match client responses back to their corresponding server requests. The `OutgoingMessageSender` acts as the central hub for all outbound communication, while `ThreadScopedOutgoingMessageSender` provides a scoped view for sending to a specific set of connections.

This file manages the runtime state of individual conversation threads in the app server, tracking which client connections are subscribed to updates from each thread and coordinating cancellation/interruption signals across them. The `ThreadState` struct accumulates per-turn metadata like errors and file changes, while the `ThreadStateManager` maintains the bidirectional mapping between connections and threads so that server events can be properly routed to interested clients and cleanup can cascade when connections or threads are removed.

This file implements the network transport layer for the app-server, handling both stdio and WebSocket communication with clients. It defines how incoming JSON-RPC messages are parsed and routed to the message processor, how outgoing messages are serialized and sent to clients, and includes backpressure handling (returning "overloaded" errors when queues fill up). The file also manages connection lifecycle (opening, closing, and tracking connected clients) and supports per-connection filtering to skip notifications that clients have opted out of.

This file serves as the entry point for the integration test suite, aggregating all test modules located in the `tests/suite/` directory into a single test binary. It allows the test framework to discover and run all tests defined across multiple module files as a cohesive unit.

This file provides test fixtures for creating fake ChatGPT authentication credentials. It includes a builder pattern for constructing test auth objects with customizable tokens, claims, and metadata, along with utilities to encode those claims into JWT format and write them to disk as auth.json files. This enables tests to simulate authenticated ChatGPT sessions without requiring real credentials.

This test utility generates a mock `config.toml` file for integration tests, allowing tests to configure feature flags, model providers, authentication requirements, and other server settings without needing a real configuration. It programmatically constructs the TOML structure by assembling feature entries and provider-specific blocks based on the test parameters passed in.

This file serves as a test utilities library that aggregates and re-exports common testing fixtures and helpers for the app-server tests. It provides builders and factories for setting up mock authentication, configuration, MCP processes, model servers, and various response types needed across integration tests, along with a helper function to deserialize JSONRPC responses.

This file provides a test harness for spawning and communicating with the `codex-app-server` process via JSON-RPC over stdin/stdout. It handles the full lifecycle of the server—initialization, sending requests for all supported operations (conversations, threads, authentication, configuration, etc.), reading responses, and buffering out-of-order messages. The class is designed to let integration tests drive the server through its protocol in a controlled way.

This file provides test utilities for creating mock API servers that simulate the Codex model's `/v1/responses` endpoint. It offers three mock server variants: one that returns a sequence of predefined responses in order with call count validation, one that does the same without enforcing the number of calls, and one that repeats the same assistant message for every request. These mocks enable integration tests to verify app-server behavior against a controlled, predictable model API without hitting a real service.

This test utility creates a mock models cache file to prevent the ModelsManager from making network requests during testing. It converts the application's built-in model presets into cached ModelInfo entries and writes them to `models_cache.json` in the test's codex home directory, allowing tests to work with a static set of models without external dependencies.

This file provides test helper functions that construct mock Server-Sent Event (SSE) responses simulating various tool calls and messages that the Codex assistant might generate. It enables integration tests to verify the app-server's behavior without hitting real APIs, covering shell command execution, patch application, user input requests, and assistant messages.

This test utility module creates fake rollout (session recording) files for testing purposes. It provides functions to generate minimal JSONL-formatted session files under the standard `CODEX_HOME/sessions/YYYY/MM/DD/` directory structure, with support for customizing timestamps, preview text, model providers, and git metadata. The module exists to enable integration tests to set up realistic session data without manually constructing the complex file formats and directory hierarchies that the app-server expects.

This file tests the conversation archival workflow—verifying that when a user archives a conversation thread, its rollout (execution record) is correctly moved from the active sessions directory to an archived sessions subdirectory. It sets up a mock server, creates a conversation, sends a user message to materialize the rollout file, then archives it and asserts the file has been relocated as expected.

This test suite validates the authentication system for the Codex app server, specifically testing how the server handles authentication status queries and API key logins under various configurations. It covers scenarios like checking auth status with no credentials, logging in via API key, respecting forced login methods, and controlling token visibility in responses. The tests ensure the auth system correctly enforces configuration rules (such as required vs. optional authentication) and properly rejects login attempts when they conflict with server settings.

This file contains integration tests for the Codex message processor that validate end-to-end JSON-RPC conversation flows between a client and the MCP server. The tests verify that conversations can be created, messages sent, command approvals handled, and sandbox/working directory settings updated across multiple turns, using a mock model server to simulate AI responses. Each test exercises different aspects of the protocol—from basic message handling to approval policy enforcement and per-turn configuration changes—ensuring the server correctly processes and routes requests through the conversation pipeline.

This file contains integration tests for the app server's configuration loading system. It verifies that the server correctly parses user configuration files (TOML format) and responds to configuration requests via JSON-RPC, testing both a fully-populated config with various settings like sandbox modes, model parameters, and tool preferences, as well as an empty config to ensure graceful handling of missing settings.

This is an integration test that verifies the core workflow of creating a conversation thread and sending a user message through the Codex app server's JSON-RPC API. It sets up a mock model server, initializes an MCP process, creates a new conversation, adds a listener, sends a user message, and validates that the request was correctly forwarded to the backend with the expected model and message content.

This integration test verifies that the fork conversation feature correctly creates a new rollout file with a fresh conversation ID while preserving the original conversation's state and respecting parameter overrides like model selection. It validates the end-to-end behavior including the sessionConfigured notification, the fork response, and that the original rollout remains unmodified.

This test suite validates the fuzzy file search functionality of the Codex app server. It verifies that file search results are properly sorted by relevance score, include character match indices for highlighting, and support request cancellation via tokens. The tests use temporary file systems and an MCP server process to ensure the search behaves deterministically across different file hierarchies.

This is an integration test that verifies the interrupt functionality of the Codex app server. It validates that a long-running shell command can be successfully interrupted mid-execution by sending an `InterruptConversation` request, and that the server correctly reports the abort reason. The test sets up a mock model provider, starts a conversation, sends a 10-second sleep command, interrupts it after 1 second, and confirms the interruption was acknowledged.

This file contains integration tests for the conversation listing and resuming functionality in the Codex app server. It verifies that the server correctly lists conversations with pagination and filtering by model provider, and that conversations can be resumed from stored rollouts, by conversation ID, or with explicit history. The tests use a temporary home directory with fake conversation files to ensure the server properly handles sorting, filtering, pagination cursors, and session initialization with various resume parameters.

This test suite validates the authentication and login functionality of the Codex app server, including logout operations, forced login method enforcement, and workspace restrictions. It uses an in-process mock server to verify that auth credentials are properly stored and removed, that login attempts respect configuration-level constraints, and that workspace IDs are correctly propagated to authentication URLs.

This file is a test suite module index that organizes integration tests for the app server into logical groups, covering functionality like authentication, thread management, messaging, search, user operations, and API versioning. It serves as the central hub that declares and exposes all test modules to the test runner.

This file contains integration tests that verify the app server correctly handles JSON schema output constraints when sending user turns to the AI model. The tests confirm that output schemas are properly formatted and passed to the backend API, and that schemas can be customized per turn rather than being conversation-wide settings. It uses a mock server to simulate OpenAI API responses and validates the exact request payloads sent.

This file contains integration tests for the `sendUserMessage` RPC method in the Codex app server. It verifies that users can successfully send messages within conversations, receive appropriate responses from a mocked AI model backend, and that the system correctly handles edge cases like session-not-found errors and model mismatches during conversation resumption. The tests also validate that raw response item notifications can be opted into via the conversation listener API and that model-switch messages are appended exactly once when resuming a conversation with a different model.

This test file validates that the "set default model" RPC endpoint correctly persists model configuration changes to the `config.toml` file. It spins up an app server process, sends a request to change the default model, and verifies that the updated configuration is actually written and saved to disk.

This test verifies that the app server correctly constructs and returns a user agent string containing system information (OS type, version, architecture) and client identifiers. It validates the `GetUserAgent` RPC endpoint by spawning a test server process, sending a request, and asserting the response matches the expected format.

This test file verifies that the user info endpoint correctly retrieves and returns the user's email address from stored authentication credentials. It sets up a test environment with mock auth data, initializes a server process, sends a user info request, and asserts that the response contains the expected email address.

# Summary

This file contains integration tests for account management functionality in the app server, including login, logout, and authentication workflows. It tests various authentication modes (API key and ChatGPT tokens), account state persistence, token refresh behavior when external auth is required, and error handling for malformed or mismatched credentials. The tests verify that the server correctly notifies clients of account changes, enforces forced login method constraints, and handles edge cases like cancelled logins and unauthorized responses.

This test suite verifies that the app server's OpenTelemetry analytics initialization respects both explicit configuration settings and default flag behavior. It ensures metrics are correctly disabled when analytics are unset and the default flag is false, and enabled when the default flag is true, providing confidence that telemetry opt-in/opt-out logic works as intended.

This file contains integration tests for the app listing functionality in the Codex app server. It validates how the system retrieves, caches, merges, and paginates lists of available applications (connectors) from multiple sources, including behavior around feature flags, thread-specific access, and resilience when remote sources fail. The tests set up mock servers that simulate both the directory API and MCP protocol endpoints to ensure the app listing system correctly combines results, emits update notifications, and handles authentication and delays.

This test file validates that the app server's collaboration mode list endpoint returns the correct default presets (plan and default modes) with their expected model and reasoning effort configurations. It exists to keep the API contract visible and catch regressions if the default collaboration mode settings are inadvertently changed.

This file contains end-to-end integration tests for the context compaction feature, which summarizes conversation history when token limits are reached. It tests both automatic compaction (triggered when conversations exceed a token threshold) and manual compaction (via explicit API call), verifying that the system correctly emits `item/started` and `item/completed` notifications and properly handles invalid or unknown thread IDs. The tests use mock HTTP servers and a local MCP process to simulate real-world compaction flows across different provider configurations (local mock and remote OpenAI).

This test suite validates the configuration management RPC endpoints for the Codex app server. It tests reading configuration from multiple layered sources (user, project, system, and managed configs), writing individual and batch config values, version conflict detection, and merging strategies. The tests ensure that configuration values are correctly resolved from their source layers and that write operations properly update and track config file changes.

This test suite validates that the WebSocket transport layer correctly isolates connections and routes messages independently. It spawns a real app-server instance, establishes multiple concurrent WebSocket connections, and verifies that initialization state doesn't leak between connections and that identical request IDs on different connections are routed to their respective clients without interference.

This file contains integration tests for the dynamic tools feature in the app server. It verifies that dynamic tool specifications (custom tools provided at runtime) are correctly serialized into model requests, that the server can invoke these tools and receive responses from clients, and that tool outputs with various content types (text, images) are properly forwarded to the language model. The tests simulate the full lifecycle of a dynamic tool call: defining tools during thread initialization, triggering a tool call during conversation, handling the client response, and validating that the output reaches the model in the expected format.

This test suite validates that the experimental API capability flag is properly enforced across the app server. It verifies that requests to experimental methods and fields are rejected when clients don't declare the `experimental_api` capability during initialization, while ensuring non-experimental operations continue to work regardless of this flag setting.

This test verifies that the experimental feature list API endpoint correctly returns all features with their metadata and lifecycle stage information. It initializes a test server, sends a feature list request, and asserts that the response contains the expected feature data (names, stages, descriptions, and enabled status) derived from the core features registry.

This test suite validates the initialization protocol for the Codex app server, specifically ensuring that client information is properly captured, validated, and used to construct the user agent identifier. The tests verify that the originator name derives from client info by default, can be overridden via environment variable, rejects invalid HTTP header characters in client names, and that clients can opt out of specific notification types during initialization.

This is a test suite module index that organizes integration tests for the v2 API endpoints. It aggregates test modules covering core functionality like thread management (start, resume, fork, archive), turn operations (start, steer, interrupt), user interactions (account, collaboration, input requests), system features (rate limits, websocket connections, compaction), and API metadata (model lists, skills, config). The file serves as the namespace and discovery point for all v2 API test suites.

This file contains integration tests for the model listing API endpoint, verifying that the server correctly returns available AI models with proper pagination support and error handling. The tests validate three key behaviors: returning all models when requested with a large limit, correctly paginating through models one at a time with cursor tokens, and rejecting requests with malformed cursor values with appropriate error responses.

This file tests that the app server correctly handles JSON Schema output constraints in turn requests and properly forwards them to the model provider. It verifies both that a single turn can specify an output schema and that different turns within the same thread can have different (or no) output schemas, ensuring the constraints are applied per-turn rather than globally.

This file contains integration tests for the plan mode feature in the Codex app server. It verifies that when a turn is executed in plan mode, the server correctly extracts plan content from XML-style `<proposed_plan>` blocks in the assistant's response and emits it as a separate `ThreadItem::Plan` through delta notifications, while also validating that responses without plan blocks don't incorrectly create plan items.

This file contains integration tests for the rate limits API endpoint, verifying authentication requirements and response correctness. It tests three scenarios: that unauthenticated requests are rejected, that API key-only authentication (without ChatGPT credentials) is insufficient, and that fully authenticated requests correctly retrieve and parse rate limit data including primary/secondary windows and plan-specific limits. The tests mock the OpenAI API backend to validate the server's ability to fetch and transform rate limit information.

This integration test validates the complete flow of requesting user input during a conversation turn. It simulates a client sending a turn start request, verifies the server generates a `ToolRequestUserInput` request with the correct parameters, and confirms the system properly handles the user's response and completes the turn. The test uses mocked responses and a local MCP process to ensure the request-response cycle works end-to-end.

This file contains integration tests for the code review functionality of the Codex application server. It validates that review requests are properly initiated, executed, and return structured review findings with appropriate delivery modes (inline vs. detached), while also ensuring input validation rejects malformed requests like empty commit SHAs or branch names.

This test suite validates the skills listing functionality of the app server, specifically focusing on how per-working-directory extra user root paths are discovered and cached. It verifies that extra skill roots are correctly included when specified, rejects invalid relative paths, ignores extra roots for unrelated working directories, and ensures that caching behavior respects the force_reload flag to pick up newly added skills.

This test file validates the thread archive feature's behavior, specifically ensuring that archiving a thread requires its rollout (underlying data) to be materialized first. It verifies that archive requests fail with an appropriate error when attempted on unmaterialized threads, succeed after a user interaction materializes the rollout, and correctly move the thread file to an archived directory upon success.

This test suite validates the thread forking functionality in the Codex app server. It verifies that forking a persisted conversation thread creates an independent copy with a new ID while preserving the original thread's state, and that attempting to fork an unmaterialized (in-memory) thread properly fails with an error. The tests use mock infrastructure to simulate the model server and filesystem interactions.

This file is a comprehensive integration test suite for the thread listing API endpoint. It validates that the server correctly retrieves, filters, paginates, and sorts conversation threads based on various criteria like provider, source kind, timestamps, and archive status. The tests ensure pagination works correctly with cursors, limits are enforced, filtering by model provider and session source types functions properly, sorting by creation and modification time works as expected, and edge cases like tie-breaking by UUID and invalid cursors are handled appropriately.

This file contains integration tests for the `thread_loaded_list` API endpoint, which retrieves a list of currently loaded thread IDs. It validates two key behaviors: that the endpoint correctly returns thread IDs that have been started, and that it properly handles pagination when multiple threads exist. The tests use a mock MCP process and mock responses server to simulate realistic server interactions without external dependencies.

This file contains integration tests for the thread read functionality of the Codex app server. It validates that the `ThreadRead` RPC method correctly retrieves thread metadata and conversation history, testing scenarios like reading without turns, including turn data, handling unmaterialized threads, and returning precomputed paths before thread materialization.

This file contains integration tests for the thread resume functionality in the Codex app server. It validates that the `ThreadResumeRequest` API correctly loads existing conversation history, preserves file metadata when appropriate, handles parameter overrides (model, personality, explicit history), and properly fails when required MCP servers are unavailable. The tests ensure thread resumption works whether threads are looked up by ID or file path, and that metadata timestamps are only updated when actual modifications occur.

This integration test verifies that the thread rollback feature correctly removes the last N turns from a conversation thread and persists the changes. It creates a multi-turn conversation, rolls back the most recent turn, and confirms that both the immediate rollback response and a subsequent resume operation reflect the pruned history.

This test suite validates the thread initialization API (ThreadStart) in the Codex application server. It verifies that threads are correctly created with proper metadata, that configuration settings from project directories are respected, that ephemeral threads don't expose filesystem paths, and that initialization failures are properly reported when required MCP servers fail to start. The tests use mock servers and temporary directories to simulate realistic scenarios without external dependencies.

This test validates that the thread unarchive operation correctly moves a archived thread back into the active sessions directory and updates its metadata. It creates a thread, archives it (moving it to an archive location), then unarchives it and verifies the thread is restored to its original path with an updated timestamp.

This test validates that the turn interrupt feature correctly aborts long-running operations in the v2 API. It spins up a mock server and MCP process, starts a thread and turn with a long-running sleep command, then sends an interrupt request and verifies the turn transitions to an `Interrupted` status with a corresponding notification.

This file contains integration tests for the `turn/start` RPC endpoint in the Codex app server, validating how the server handles user requests to start conversational turns within threads. The tests verify core functionality including request headers, notification emissions, parameter overrides (model, collaboration mode, personality), approval workflows for commands and file changes, sandbox policy handling, and various edge cases like mid-thread personality changes and declined approvals. Each test sets up a mock server, initializes the MCP client, and exercises different aspects of the turn lifecycle to ensure the server behaves correctly under various configurations and user inputs.

This test suite validates the turn steering functionality in the Codex app server, which allows users to inject new input into an ongoing conversation turn. The tests verify two critical behaviors: that steering requests are rejected when no active turn exists, and that valid steering requests correctly return the ID of the active turn being modified. These tests ensure the turn steering API properly validates state and integrates with the broader thread and turn management system.

This file parses and validates `apply_patch` command invocations from shell scripts, particularly those using bash heredocs. It extracts patch content from various shell forms (direct invocation, bash/PowerShell/cmd heredocs with optional `cd` prefixes), validates the patch syntax, and resolves file paths relative to the specified working directory. The core logic uses Tree-sitter to parse bash scripts with strict query matching to ensure only intended command patterns are recognized, preventing accidental matches on malformed or unrelated scripts.

This library implements a patch application system that parses and applies file modifications to a filesystem. It takes patch descriptions (in a custom format with sections like `*** Add File`, `*** Update File`, `*** Delete File`) and executes them—creating, modifying, or removing files as specified. The core logic handles matching old content in files against patch chunks (handling edge cases like Unicode normalization and end-of-file boundaries), computing line-by-line replacements, and generating unified diffs to show what changed. It's designed to work as a tool that AI models (like GPT-4) can invoke to make file system changes based on patch instructions.

This is the binary entry point that delegates to the actual implementation in the `codex_apply_patch` library crate. It exists to provide an executable command-line interface while keeping the core logic in a reusable library module.

This parser validates and converts patch text into structured hunks (add, delete, or update operations on files) according to a custom patch format used by OpenAI Codex. It exists to handle an intentional quirk where GPT-4.1 sometimes wraps patches in shell heredoc syntax (`<<EOF...EOF`), so the parser includes lenient mode to strip those markers and process the actual patch content beneath them. The module does not attempt to verify patches can actually be applied to a filesystem—it only validates the format and extracts the logical operations.

This module finds a sequence of pattern lines within a larger block of lines, using progressively lenient matching strategies to handle common whitespace and Unicode variations. It's essential for applying patches to source files where minor formatting differences (trailing spaces, fancy quotes, typographic dashes) shouldn't prevent a patch from being applied, mirroring behavior similar to `git apply`. The function tries exact matches first, then relaxes matching to ignore trailing whitespace, then both leading and trailing whitespace, and finally normalizes Unicode punctuation to ASCII equivalents.

This file provides a standalone executable entry point for the apply_patch tool, handling command-line argument parsing and I/O setup. It accepts a patch payload either as a command-line argument or via stdin, validates the input, and delegates the actual patch application to the core `apply_patch` function while managing exit codes and output streams.

This file serves as the entry point for the test suite, aggregating all integration tests from the `tests/suite/` directory into a single test binary. It allows Rust's test runner to discover and execute all modularized tests from one location.

This file contains integration tests for the `apply_patch` CLI tool, verifying that it can correctly add and update files through both command-line arguments and stdin input. The tests use temporary directories and custom patch syntax to ensure the tool produces expected file changes and success messages, serving as end-to-end validation of the CLI's core functionality.

This file serves as the test module organizer for the apply-patch crate, aggregating test suites across different concerns: command-line interface testing, various patch application scenarios, and platform-specific tooling tests (excluding Windows). It acts as a central entry point that makes all nested test modules discoverable by the Rust test harness.

This file implements an integration test suite for the `apply-patch` tool by running scenario-based tests. Each scenario consists of an input directory, a patch file, and an expected output directory; the test executes apply-patch on a copy of the input, then verifies the resulting filesystem state matches the expected state exactly. This approach allows testing the patch application tool against realistic, multi-file scenarios without asserting on exit codes, focusing instead on the correctness of the final output.

This file contains integration tests for the `apply_patch` command-line tool, validating its ability to parse and execute patch operations on a filesystem. The tests cover the full range of patch functionality including file creation, deletion, modification, and relocation, as well as error cases like missing files, invalid patch syntax, and malformed hunks. Each test uses temporary directories to isolate filesystem changes and verify both successful operations and appropriate failure messages.

This file implements the "arg0 trick" to dispatch different CLI commands from a single Codex executable based on how it was invoked. It creates temporary symlinks (or batch scripts on Windows) for helper commands like `apply_patch` and `codex-linux-sandbox`, injects them into the PATH, and routes invocations to the appropriate handler. The module also loads environment variables from `~/.codex/.env` before runtime initialization and manages cleanup of stale temporary directories using file locking.

This module provides a trait extension that wraps any async future with cancellation support via a `CancellationToken`. The `OrCancelExt` trait allows futures to be interrupted cleanly—returning a result if the future completes first, or an error if the cancellation token is triggered first. It's a utility for gracefully handling timeouts and shutdown signals in async Rust code.

This file implements an HTTP client for communicating with OpenAI's Codex backend services, abstracting away the differences between two API styles (the `/api/codex` paths and the ChatGPT `/wham` paths). It handles authentication via bearer tokens and account IDs, constructs properly formatted requests with appropriate headers, and provides high-level methods for common operations like listing tasks, fetching task details, checking rate limits, and creating new tasks. The client also transforms rate limit and credit responses from the backend into a normalized internal format for consumption by other parts of the system.

This is the public API entry point for a backend client library that provides type definitions and a client interface for interacting with a code task management system. It re-exports the main `Client` class along with several response and data types related to tasks, configurations, and turn attempts, establishing what functionality is exposed to external consumers of this crate.

This file defines the data types for deserializing Cloud Tasks task-detail responses from the backend API, along with a trait that provides convenient methods to extract specific information (diffs, messages, prompts, errors) from the response. It exists because the auto-generated OpenAPI models were inadequate, so custom hand-rolled types were needed to properly parse and work with the task execution data.

This file implements a CLI command that retrieves a completed Codex agent task and applies its generated diff to the local filesystem. It fetches the task by ID, extracts the diff output from the task's PR turn, and uses git to apply the patch, reporting success or failure with detailed error information.

This module provides HTTP client utilities for making authenticated requests to the ChatGPT backend API. It handles token initialization, constructs requests with proper authentication headers (bearer token and account ID), and manages response parsing with optional request timeouts. The functions serve as a bridge between the Codex application and the ChatGPT backend service.

This file manages a global in-memory cache for ChatGPT authentication tokens. It provides thread-safe getter and setter functions for token data, and includes an initialization function that loads the token from the auth.json configuration file during startup. The cached token is stored in a lazy-initialized static variable protected by a read-write lock.

This file manages the discovery and caching of ChatGPT connectors (third-party integrations or apps). It fetches connector metadata from a directory API and workspace-specific sources, merges the results, applies filtering and normalization rules, and maintains an in-memory cache to avoid repeated requests. The module also handles accessibility markers to indicate which connectors users can actually use, and blocks certain internal or deprecated connectors from appearing in listings.

This file defines the data structures and API client function for retrieving task information from a backend service, specifically extracting PR diff outputs from assistant responses. The `get_task` function makes an HTTP request to fetch task details by ID, while the associated structs deserialize the nested JSON response to isolate the diff content needed for further processing.

This file serves as the public module interface for a ChatGPT integration library, exposing key submodules for applying commands, managing connectors, and retrieving tasks while keeping internal implementation details like the client and token handling private.

This file serves as the entry point for the integration test suite, aggregating all test modules that live in the `tests/suite/` directory. It allows multiple related tests to be compiled and run together as a single test binary rather than as separate test crates.

This end-to-end test suite validates the `apply_diff_from_task` function by verifying it correctly applies code changes to a git repository. It tests two scenarios: successful application of a diff that creates a new fibonacci.js file with expected content, and graceful handling of merge conflicts when the target file already exists with conflicting changes. The tests use temporary git repositories and fixture data to simulate real-world usage without external dependencies.

This file serves as a test suite module aggregator that brings together integration tests previously organized as standalone files. By centralizing test module imports here, it provides a single entry point for discovering and running all end-to-end and integration tests for the ChatGPT component.

Defines the CLI command for launching Codex Desktop, specifying which workspace path to open and allowing an optional override for the macOS application download URL. On macOS, it resolves the provided path and delegates to the desktop app installer/launcher.

This file provides CLI debugging commands to execute arbitrary commands within sandboxed environments across different platforms (macOS Seatbelt, Linux Landlock, and Windows). It handles configuration loading, environment setup, and dispatches to the appropriate platform-specific sandbox implementation, with optional denial logging on macOS to help developers understand sandbox policy violations.

This file provides macOS process tracking functionality for the debug sandbox, using `kqueue` to monitor a parent process and all its recursive descendants. It detects when child processes are forked, executed, or exit, maintaining a complete set of all PIDs that have ever been spawned under a root process. This is primarily used to ensure proper cleanup of all spawned processes in the debugging environment.

This file implements sandbox denial logging for macOS, capturing and parsing security policy violations when child processes attempt restricted operations. It spawns Apple's `log` command to stream sandbox events, associates denials with tracked process IDs, and extracts structured capability denial information from the log output to report back to callers.

This module handles launching the Codex Desktop application on macOS, either by opening an existing installation or by downloading and installing it first. It manages the complete installation workflow including DMG download, mounting, app bundle extraction, and copying to the Applications folder, while also providing fallback locations and robust error handling for each step.

This module provides a cross-platform entry point for launching or installing a desktop application, with platform-specific implementations delegated to OS-specific submodules. Currently, it only implements macOS support via a `mac` submodule, but the structure allows for adding Windows and Linux implementations in the future.

This module provides platform-specific logic to translate a child process's exit status into the CLI's own exit code. On Unix systems, it preserves explicit exit codes and converts signal terminations into the standard 128+signal convention, while on Windows it simply passes through the exit code or defaults to 1 if unavailable.

This file defines the command-line interface structures for Codex's sandbox execution modes across different operating systems (macOS via Seatbelt, Linux via Landlock, and Windows). Each command struct captures platform-specific sandboxing options, including a `full-auto` convenience flag for easy network-disabled sandboxed execution, and accepts arbitrary command arguments to run within the respective sandbox environment. It serves as the entry point for CLI argument parsing that routes user input to the appropriate platform sandbox implementation.

This file implements the CLI authentication commands for the Codex client, supporting multiple login methods including ChatGPT OAuth, API key authentication, and device code flow. It provides functions to authenticate users, check login status, and logout, while respecting server-side configuration that may restrict which login methods are available. The code handles credential storage, detects headless environments for appropriate auth flow selection, and masks sensitive API keys in user-facing output.

# main.rs

This file is the entry point for the Codex CLI application, orchestrating a multi-tool command dispatcher. It defines the top-level CLI structure using clap, parses arguments, and routes execution to various subcommands (interactive TUI, exec, login, MCP server, app server, sandbox tools, etc.). The file also handles configuration merging, feature flag management, and terminal compatibility checks, ultimately delegating to specialized modules for each command's implementation.

This file implements the CLI interface for managing Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers in Codex. It provides subcommands to list, view, add, remove, and authenticate with MCP servers, storing their configurations (like launch commands or HTTP URLs) in the global config file. The code handles both stdio-based servers launched locally and streamable HTTP servers, plus OAuth login/logout flows for authentication.

This module handles path conversion between Windows and WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux) formats, enabling the CLI to work seamlessly when running under WSL. It provides utilities to detect if the process is running in WSL and automatically convert Windows-style paths (like `C:\foo\bar`) to their WSL equivalents (`/mnt/c/foo/bar`), with a normalization function that conditionally applies the conversion only when needed.

This file contains integration tests for the `execpolicy check` CLI command, verifying that the policy evaluation engine correctly matches command patterns against rules and returns the expected JSON output. The tests validate both basic rule matching (where a git push command is forbidden) and the inclusion of justification messages when rules provide them, ensuring the command's behavior matches the specification.

Integration tests for the Codex CLI's feature flag management system. These tests verify that the `features enable` and `features disable` commands correctly write feature flags to the config.toml file and produce appropriate user-facing messages, including warnings for under-development features.

This integration test file validates the CLI commands for adding and removing MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers in the Codex application. It tests that the `mcp add` and `mcp remove` commands correctly update the global configuration file, handle various transport types (stdio and HTTP), manage environment variables, and properly reject invalid argument combinations. The tests ensure the CLI interface behaves correctly across different server configuration scenarios.

Integration tests for the `codex mcp list` and `codex mcp get` CLI commands that verify MCP server configuration is displayed correctly in both human-readable and JSON formats. The tests validate empty states, output rendering with masked environment variables, and the handling of disabled servers across different output modes.

This crate fetches configuration requirements from a backend server for Business and Enterprise ChatGpt customers, caching the results locally with cryptographic signatures to detect tampering. It implements retry logic with exponential backoff, validates cache entries by user identity and expiration time, and gracefully degrades to running without cloud requirements on fetch failure or timeout.

This file defines the core API contracts and data types for a cloud-based task management system that appears to handle code generation and patching workflows. It establishes the `CloudBackend` trait that abstracts communication with a backend service, along with supporting types like `TaskSummary`, `ApplyOutcome`, and `TurnAttempt` that represent tasks, their statuses, and the results of applying generated code changes. The types support features like diff tracking, multiple assistant attempts, and dry-run validation before applying patches to a working tree.

This file implements an HTTP client for interacting with a cloud-based task management backend. It wraps a lower-level backend client and exposes operations for listing tasks, retrieving task details (summaries, diffs, messages), creating new tasks, listing sibling attempts, and applying or preflight-checking git patches. The client parses JSON responses from the backend API and maps them into domain types, handles various response formats and fallback strategies for extracting data, and logs operations and errors to a local `error.log` file for debugging.

This library provides a public API for interacting with cloud-based task management, exposing core types like `TaskId`, `TaskStatus`, and `CloudBackend` along with result and error types. It conditionally includes either a mock implementation or an HTTP client depending on the build feature, allowing the same interface to work in both testing and production environments.

This file provides a mock implementation of the `CloudBackend` trait for testing purposes. It simulates cloud task management operations by returning hardcoded task data with different scenarios per environment, allowing developers to test client code without making actual cloud API calls. The mock includes realistic sample diffs and supports task listing, retrieval, application, and attempt tracking with minimal dependencies.

This file defines the core data models and state management for a terminal UI application that manages cloud-based code generation tasks. It contains the main `App` struct that tracks UI state (selected task, modal windows, loading spinners), modal-specific states for filtering environments and applying changes, and a `DiffOverlay` struct for displaying task details and diffs. The file also defines `AppEvent` as an event type for asynchronous background operations (loading tasks, applying patches, detecting environments) to keep the UI responsive, and includes a `load_tasks` utility function that fetches filtered task lists from a cloud backend with timeout handling.

This file defines the CLI argument structure and subcommands for the Codex Cloud Tasks tool, enabling users to submit tasks for remote execution, monitor their progress, and apply results locally. It uses `clap` for parsing five main operations: `exec` (submit a task), `status` (check task progress), `list` (browse tasks), `apply` (integrate results), and `diff` (preview changes), each with their own validated parameters like environment ID, attempt limits, and pagination options.

This module automatically detects and lists available code environments by querying a backend API. It first tries to find environments specific to the current Git repository (by parsing GitHub origins), then falls back to the global environment list. The selection logic prioritizes user-specified labels, pinned environments, and task count as tiebreakers. This enables the Cloud Tasks feature to intelligently choose a working environment without requiring manual user configuration.

# Summary

This is the main entry point and orchestration layer for the `codex cloud` CLI and TUI application, which allows users to create, view, manage, and apply code generation tasks to a remote backend. It handles both command-line subcommands (exec, status, list, diff, apply) and a full interactive terminal UI with task browsing, environment selection, diff viewing, and apply workflows. The file ties together authentication, HTTP backend communication, git branch resolution, event-driven state management, and a ratatui-based UI with non-blocking I/O.

Defines the UI state and composition for a "New Task" page in a terminal interface, managing user input through a text composer along with task configuration like environment selection and attempt count. It provides a structured container for collecting task creation parameters before submission.

This file implements a scrollable text view component for displaying diffs or messages with word-wrapping. It manages raw content lines, caches wrapped versions for a given display width, and tracks scroll position—automatically rewrapping when width changes and clamping scroll state to valid bounds. The wrapping logic respects Unicode character widths, converts tabs to spaces, and breaks lines at whitespace or punctuation to keep text readable within viewport constraints.

This file renders the terminal user interface for a Cloud Tasks management application using the Ratatui TUI framework. It displays a task list with filtering/sorting, handles multiple modal overlays (environment selection, parallel attempts, apply confirmation), and renders detailed views of task diffs and conversation history with syntax highlighting. The UI adapts dynamically—dimming the background when modals are active, showing spinners during async operations, and allowing users to navigate through task attempts and view detailed execution results.

This utility module provides helper functions for the cloud tasks TUI component to interact with the ChatGPT backend API. It handles URL normalization for different ChatGPT hosts, JWT token parsing to extract account IDs, authentication header construction (User-Agent, Authorization, ChatGPT-Account-Id), and relative time formatting for display purposes. The module essentially centralizes common operations needed to build authenticated requests to ChatGPT and present task information to users.

This test verifies that the mock cloud tasks client correctly filters and returns different task lists based on environment parameters. It ensures the MockClient backend behaves as expected by validating that querying with no environment returns a default set of tasks, while specifying "env-A" or "env-B" returns environment-specific task subsets.

This module defines the authentication abstraction layer for the Codex API client. It provides an `AuthProvider` trait that sources bearer tokens and account IDs, along with utility functions to inject these credentials into HTTP request headers. The trait is designed to be lightweight and synchronous, expecting any async credential refresh to happen upstream before requests are constructed.

This file defines the canonical data structures and request/response types for the Codex API's Responses endpoints. It serves as a shared vocabulary layer that bridges between the internal protocol models and the public-facing API, including input payloads for operations like compaction and memory summarization, event types for streaming responses, reasoning and text formatting controls, and WebSocket request wrappers for the Responses API.

This file implements a stream adapter that consolidates streaming token deltas from the API into complete assistant messages. Rather than emitting individual text delta events, `AggregatedStream` accumulates text and reasoning content incrementally, then emits them as single unified `OutputItemDone` events when the response completes. This simplifies downstream consumption of streaming responses by batching partial updates into complete messages.

This file provides a client for calling the "responses/compact" API endpoint, which compacts response histories by sending them to the server and receiving a condensed set of response items back. It wraps HTTP transport and authentication logic to handle serialization, request execution, and error handling for the compaction operation.

This file implements a client for the memories API endpoint, specifically handling trace summarization requests. It provides a `MemoriesClient` that wraps HTTP transport and authentication to send memory data to a remote service and parse summarized outputs. The module exists to abstract away the details of making trace summarization API calls, allowing other parts of the codebase to summarize execution traces without managing HTTP requests directly.

This module file organizes the endpoint layer of the Codex API by re-exporting sub-modules that handle different aspects of API request/response processing—including data aggregation, compaction, memory management, model operations, and WebSocket communication—along with internal session management logic.

This file implements a client for fetching available AI models from the OpenAI API. The `ModelsClient` makes GET requests to the `/models` endpoint, parses the response into a list of `ModelInfo` objects, and extracts the ETag header for caching purposes. It exists to provide a clean, type-safe interface for model enumeration with support for client versioning and telemetry tracking.

This file implements the client for OpenAI's Responses API endpoint, handling streaming requests to generate completions. It manages HTTP request construction (headers, compression, conversation context), authentication, and spawns server-sent event streams to deliver responses back to the caller, with optional telemetry instrumentation.

This file implements a WebSocket client for streaming responses from the OpenAI Codex API. It establishes WebSocket connections, handles the bidirectional message exchange (sending requests and receiving streamed response events), manages connection state and timeouts, and gracefully converts WebSocket errors and server-side error events into the application's error types. The code uses a pump-based architecture to separate command sending from message receiving, enabling concurrent operations while maintaining proper connection lifecycle management.

`EndpointSession` is a session handler that encapsulates HTTP transport, authentication, and provider configuration to execute API requests with consistent telemetry and retry logic. It provides methods to make both standard and streaming requests while automatically injecting auth headers, building provider-specific requests, and wrapping execution with observability instrumentation.

Defines the error types that can occur when calling the Codex API, encompassing transport failures, HTTP errors, rate limiting, quota issues, and retryable conditions. This enum serves as the central error contract for the API layer, allowing callers to distinguish between different failure modes and handle them appropriately (e.g., respecting rate limit delays or retrying transient errors).

This is the public API facade for the Codex API library, re-exporting core types and clients from internal modules to define what consumers can access. It organizes functionality into logical domains—authentication, endpoints (responses, memories, models), error handling, rate limiting, and telemetry—while hiding implementation details. This file establishes the primary entry point for downstream code using this Rust API client.

This file defines the configuration and helpers for HTTP API providers that the Codex client uses to communicate with different API deployments. The `Provider` struct encapsulates endpoint details like base URL, headers, query parameters, and retry policies, while providing utility methods to construct properly-formed requests and WebSocket URLs. It also includes Azure-specific detection logic to identify when requests are targeting Azure's OpenAI services, which may require special handling.

This module parses rate-limit and credit information from HTTP response headers and JSON event payloads, converting them into structured `RateLimitSnapshot` objects. It handles multiple rate-limit header families (identified by limit IDs), supports both primary and secondary rate-limit windows, and extracts user credit balances. The parsing logic normalizes header names and validates data, making it easy for the API client to check usage quotas and plan details in responses from the Codex service.

This file constructs and manages HTTP headers for API requests, specifically handling conversation session tracking and sub-agent source identification. It provides utilities to build header maps with conversation IDs, map internal sub-agent types to header values for routing/tracking purposes, and safely insert headers with validation.

This file serves as the module gateway for request-related functionality, re-exporting headers (for internal use) and responses (for public use) to organize request handling concerns into separate sub-modules. It allows callers to access request/response types through a clean, unified `requests` namespace rather than navigating nested module paths directly.

This file defines response handling utilities for the Codex API, specifically a compression enum and a function that enriches response payloads with item identifiers. The `attach_item_ids` function maps internal response items back to their corresponding JSON output objects and injects tracking IDs where they exist, enabling the API to maintain referential integrity between structured responses and their serialized JSON representation.

This module serves as the public interface for server-sent events (SSE) functionality, re-exporting key streaming operations from the `responses` submodule. It primarily handles streaming responses back to clients, including processing SSE data and spawning response streams, with support for fixture-based testing streams.

This file handles parsing and processing Server-Sent Events (SSE) from the Codex API response stream. It deserializes incoming SSE events into structured response events (like text deltas, reasoning content, token usage, and completion signals) and converts various error responses into appropriate API error types (rate limits, quota exceeded, context window exceeded, etc.). The module also extracts metadata from response headers and provides test utilities for fixture-based SSE stream testing.

This file provides telemetry instrumentation for the Codex API client's HTTP and WebSocket communications. It defines trait interfaces for capturing metrics on SSE polling, WebSocket events, and request/retry behavior, then wraps the core retry logic to automatically record timing and error information for each attempt. This allows callers to hook into request lifecycle events for monitoring and observability without modifying the underlying transport code.

This test file validates the behavior of the `ResponsesClient` API client, specifically testing that it correctly constructs HTTP requests with proper paths, authentication headers, retry logic, and provider-specific configurations. The file includes mock transport implementations to record and inspect outgoing requests without making real network calls, enabling isolated testing of client request construction and error handling.

This is an integration test that verifies the `ModelsClient` correctly calls the models endpoint and parses the response. It uses a mock HTTP server to simulate the API, constructs a realistic model response with reasoning levels and configuration details, then asserts that the client makes the expected GET request and deserializes the model data correctly.

This end-to-end test file validates that the `ResponsesClient` correctly parses Server-Sent Events (SSE) streams from the API and properly handles response events. It uses a mock transport to simulate SSE responses and verifies both basic event parsing (items and completion signals) and stream aggregation (combining text deltas into a single message). The tests ensure the client correctly deserializes different event types and that the aggregation extension properly concatenates streamed text fragments.

This crate serves as a wrapper around auto-generated OpenAPI model definitions, re-exporting them for use by other parts of the Codex backend. The models themselves are generated by a script that populates the `models/` directory, making this file a thin aggregation layer rather than hand-written code.

This file defines a Rust struct that models additional rate limit details from the OpenAPI specification, capturing the name of a rate limit, its associated metered feature, and optional nested rate limit status information. It was auto-generated by the OpenAPI code generator to enable serialization and deserialization of rate limit metadata in the backend API.

Defines the `CodeTaskDetailsResponse` struct, an auto-generated OpenAPI model representing the response payload for retrieving detailed information about a coding task, including the task itself and optional state about the current user turn, assistant turn, and diff task turn. This struct is primarily used for serializing/deserializing API responses.

This file defines a `ConfigFileResponse` struct that models the API response for retrieving configuration file data, including its contents, SHA256 hash, and metadata about when and by whom it was last modified. It's auto-generated from an OpenAPI specification and provides serialization/deserialization support for JSON communication with the backend API.

This file defines a Rust data structure that represents credit status information returned by the codex-backend API. It was auto-generated from an OpenAPI specification and provides serialization/deserialization support for tracking whether a user has credits, unlimited access, account balance, and approximate message counts for local and cloud operations.

This file defines a Rust struct that represents the API response for an external pull request, containing metadata linking it to an assistant interaction along with the underlying git pull request details and an optional SHA reference. It was auto-generated from an OpenAPI specification and includes serialization/deserialization support for JSON communication.

This file defines a Rust data model for git pull request objects, auto-generated from an OpenAPI specification. It provides serialization and deserialization support to exchange PR data with backend APIs, capturing core PR metadata like merge status, commit SHAs, and associated metadata while allowing optional fields to be omitted from JSON payloads.

This module file curates and re-exports a minimal set of OpenAPI-generated data model types for the workspace, including configuration responses, task management entities, pull request objects, and rate limiting structures. It was previously auto-generated but is now manually maintained to expose only the types actively used by the application, with plans to change this management approach in the future.

This file defines a data model for paginated task list responses in the OpenAPI backend. It represents a paginated result containing a vector of task items and an optional cursor for fetching subsequent pages, with automatic serialization/deserialization support via serde.

This file defines a data model for rate limit status information, auto-generated from an OpenAPI specification. It represents the current state of rate limiting with fields indicating whether a request is allowed, whether limits have been reached, and optional snapshots of primary and secondary rate limit windows. The struct is designed for serialization/deserialization with JSON APIs.

This file defines the OpenAPI model for a rate limit status response payload, auto-generated from an OpenAPI specification. It structures the data returned to clients about their current plan type, rate limits, credit usage, and any additional rate limit constraints they're subject to. The `PlanType` enum enumerates all subscription tiers from guest to enterprise that the backend supports.

Defines a data model for capturing rate limit information at a point in time, including the percentage of quota used, the duration of the rate limit window, and when the limit resets. This struct appears to be auto-generated from an OpenAPI specification and is used for serializing/deserializing rate limit status responses.

Defines the `TaskListItem` data model that represents a task entry in a list, with fields for identity, title, status metadata, timestamps, and associated pull requests. This is an auto-generated OpenAPI model struct designed for serializing and deserializing task data exchanged between the backend API and clients.

This file defines the `TaskResponse` data model that represents a task object returned by the codex-backend API. It's auto-generated from an OpenAPI specification and includes fields for task metadata like ID, title, creation timestamp, archive status, and associated pull requests, with support for JSON serialization/deserialization. The struct serves as the contract between the backend API and Rust clients consuming task data.

This file provides a wrapper around the `reqwest` HTTP client that integrates distributed tracing support via OpenTelemetry. The `CodexHttpClient` and `CodexRequestBuilder` abstractions allow fluent request construction while automatically injecting trace context headers into outgoing requests, enabling request tracking across service boundaries. It exists to ensure all HTTP calls made by the Codex client participate in the application's observability infrastructure without requiring manual trace header management at call sites.

Defines error types for the Codex client's HTTP communication layer, distinguishing between transport-level failures (HTTP errors, network issues, retries, timeouts, and request construction problems) and stream-specific failures. These error types enable callers to handle different failure modes from API requests and streaming responses appropriately.

This is the public API module for a Rust HTTP client library designed to communicate with Codex services. It re-exports core types and functions from internal submodules, providing a clean interface for request handling, error management, retry logic, server-sent events streaming, and telemetry collection. The file essentially defines what functionality external consumers of this crate can access.

Defines the core data structures for HTTP request and response handling in the Codex client. The `Request` struct encapsulates HTTP method, URL, headers, optional JSON body, and compression settings, with a builder-style API for configuration; the `Response` struct holds the status code, headers, and response body. This file serves as the foundational abstraction layer between the client logic and the underlying HTTP transport.

This file implements configurable retry logic with exponential backoff for HTTP requests. It defines a `RetryPolicy` that controls maximum retry attempts and which error types (429 rate limits, 5xx server errors, timeouts/network failures) should trigger retries, then provides a `run_with_retry` function that executes an operation with automatic retry behavior including jittered exponential backoff delays.

Provides a wrapper for consuming Server-Sent Events (SSE) streams over HTTP, parsing raw `data:` frames and forwarding them as UTF-8 strings through a channel. It handles timeouts on idle connections and propagates stream errors back to the caller, enabling the client to receive streaming responses from the OpenAI API asynchronously.
