This file implements a test utility handler that allows tool invocations to synchronize across multiple concurrent calls using barriers and controlled delays. It enables testing scenarios where multiple function calls need to coordinate their execution—for example, waiting until all participants reach a synchronization point before proceeding, with optional sleep periods before and after synchronization to simulate realistic timing conditions.

This file implements a unified handler for executing shell commands and writing to process stdin. It accepts two types of tool invocations (`exec_command` and `write_stdin`), validates whether commands are safe to run, enforces sandbox permissions and approval policies, and delegates to a process manager to execute the actual commands. The handler also intercepts patch-application commands for special handling and formats command outputs for the model.

ViewImageHandler enables the model to view local image files by validating image input support, resolving file paths, loading image content, and injecting it into the conversation context. It enforces that the model supports image inputs and verifies the specified file exists before making it available for analysis.

This file implements a JavaScript REPL (Read-Eval-Print Loop) execution environment that allows running arbitrary JavaScript code in a sandboxed Node.js process. It manages the lifecycle of a long-lived kernel process, handles communication with that kernel via JSON messages, and enforces security constraints like blocking access to sensitive Node.js built-ins and the process global. The REPL persists top-level variable bindings across multiple code executions within a single session, supports top-level await, and includes timeout protection with automatic kernel reset on execution timeouts.

This module serves as the central hub for tool execution and management in Codex. It organizes submodules for tool routing, orchestration, sandboxing, runtimes, and event handling, while providing utility functions to format command execution output for consumption by language models—including structured JSON responses with metadata, freeform text responses, and automatic truncation to respect model token budgets.

The `ToolOrchestrator` manages the execution lifecycle of tools by orchestrating three sequential stages: obtaining user approval based on policy, selecting and attempting execution in an appropriate sandbox environment, and retrying with escalated permissions if the initial sandbox denies the operation. It centralizes approval caching and retry logic to avoid re-requesting approval when escalating from a restrictive sandbox to an unrestricted one.

This file implements the execution runtime for tool calls (function invocations) within a Codex session, managing both parallel and sequential execution based on tool capabilities. It handles tool dispatch through a router, enforces mutual exclusion for non-parallel-safe tools using read/write locks, and manages cancellation and error handling including user-initiated aborts with timing information. The runtime integrates with distributed tracing for observability and wraps spawned tasks with abort-on-drop semantics for cleanup.

This file implements a central registry and dispatcher for tools (like function calls, shell commands, and MCP server tools) that the AI model can invoke. It defines the `ToolHandler` trait that tool implementations must satisfy, manages a map of available handlers, and orchestrates the execution of tool invocations with proper error handling, telemetry, sandbox policy enforcement, and hook event dispatching. The `ToolRegistryBuilder` provides a fluent API to construct the registry with tools and their specifications.

The ToolRouter acts as the central dispatcher for tool invocations in the Codex system, converting parsed tool calls from model responses into concrete executions. It maintains a registry of available tools (function-based, MCP, custom, and shell tools), determines which tools support parallel execution, and routes each tool call to the appropriate handler while gracefully converting errors into protocol-compliant response items.

This file implements the runtime executor for applying code patches that have already been verified and approved upstream. It constructs a sandboxed command to invoke `codex --codex-run-as-apply-patch` with the patch content, handles the approval workflow by caching decisions to avoid re-prompting, and executes the patch under minimal environment constraints for security and determinism.

This module provides the infrastructure for executing tools in sandboxed environments. It defines a shared helper (`build_command_spec`) to construct standardized command specifications for sandboxed execution, and includes a specialized utility (`maybe_wrap_shell_lc_with_snapshot`) that bootstraps shell commands with environment snapshots on POSIX systems, ensuring commands run with the correct shell context. The module serves as a registry and utility layer for concrete tool runtime implementations (apply_patch, shell, unified_exec) that reuse a common orchestrator for approvals, sandboxing, and retries.

This file implements the shell command execution runtime for Codex. It handles the complete lifecycle of executing shell commands: requesting user approval when needed (with caching to avoid redundant requests), constructing execution specifications with proper sandboxing permissions, and running commands in an isolated sandbox environment. The runtime supports features like PowerShell UTF-8 encoding, command snapshots, and escalation policies to balance security with functionality.

This file implements the approval and sandbox orchestration layer for unified execution requests (arbitrary commands). It defines the request structure, approval logic (including caching and user review), and delegates to a process manager to actually spawn PTYs once the request is prepared and approved. The module bridges approval/sandboxing policy with command execution.

This file defines the core approval and sandboxing infrastructure for tool execution in Codex. It provides traits and structures that control whether tools need user approval before running, how approval decisions are cached per-session, and how commands are executed within sandboxes with appropriate security policies. The `Approvable` and `Sandboxable` traits allow individual tools to customize their approval requirements and sandbox behavior, while `ToolRuntime` ties these together to orchestrate the full execution flow with user consent and environment isolation.

# Summary

This file defines the tool specification system for Codex, responsible for configuring which tools are available to AI agents based on model capabilities and feature flags. It creates tool definitions (shell, file operations, MCP integrations, collaboration, etc.), handles JSON schema validation for tool parameters, converts MCP and dynamic tools to OpenAI-compatible formats, and builds a registry that maps tool names to their handlers. The file serves as the central configuration point where tool availability is gated by feature flags (e.g., `UnifiedExec`, `JsRepl`, `Collab`) and model-specific settings.

This module provides utilities for truncating long text output to fit within token or byte budgets while preserving readable content at the beginning and end. It supports both token-based and byte-based truncation policies, handles UTF-8 boundaries correctly, and can process collections of structured output items (text and images) by intelligently dropping items and summarizing omissions when space runs out.

This file implements a tracker for accumulating file changes across multiple "turns" (rounds of edits) and generating a unified diff that reflects all modifications from the original baseline to the current state. It maintains in-memory snapshots of files when first encountered, maps file renames via internal UUIDs, and computes diffs by comparing baselines against the current disk state—handling text files with line-by-line diffs and binary files appropriately. The tracker integrates with Git to compute blob OIDs and resolve repository-relative paths for output formatting.

This module provides utilities for constructing and safely resolving per-turn metadata headers that capture git repository information (commit hash, remote URLs) and sandbox context. It enforces a centralized 250ms timeout policy across both turn execution and websocket prewarm operations to prevent metadata collection from blocking request handling, falling back gracefully when collection takes too long.

This file manages streaming output from executed commands in a unified execution system. It spawns background tasks that continuously read from a process's PTY, buffer the output as valid UTF-8, emit incremental `ExecCommandOutputDelta` events to clients (with size limits to avoid overwhelming downstream consumers), and eventually emit a final `ExecCommandEnd` event with aggregated results. The UTF-8 splitting logic ensures output streams cleanly without breaking multi-byte characters, even when handling invalid byte sequences.

Defines error types for the unified execution system that handles command execution and process management. It covers failures during process creation, stdin/stdout communication, sandbox policy violations, and mismatches between the model's session tracking and internal process IDs.

This file implements a size-capped buffer that preserves the beginning and end of a stream while discarding the middle when capacity is exceeded. It exists to efficiently limit output from long-running execution traces—for instance, capturing the first few lines and last few lines of a large log while dropping redundant middle content. The buffer splits its capacity evenly between a "head" (prefix) and "tail" (suffix), automatically tracking how many bytes were omitted in the middle for reporting purposes.

This module orchestrates interactive process execution with integrated approval workflows and sandbox enforcement. It manages long-lived PTY processes that can be reused across multiple requests (e.g., interactive shells), handles output buffering with size caps, and coordinates sandbox selection and policy enforcement through a shared `ToolOrchestrator`. When a sandbox is denied at runtime, it automatically retries without sandboxing if policy permits, keeping the user-facing approval logic centralized while isolating PTY lifecycle concerns into separate submodules.

This file manages the lifecycle and output streaming of an executed process in a sandboxed environment. It wraps a low-level process handle with async-friendly synchronization primitives (mutexes, notifications, cancellation tokens) to safely buffer process output, detect early exits, and check for sandbox permission errors. The `UnifiedExecProcess` struct serves as the primary interface for monitoring and controlling process execution while collecting output for later inspection.

This file manages the lifecycle of long-running processes in the unified execution system. It handles spawning processes with sandboxing and environment configuration, collecting output within time constraints, persisting processes for later stdin writes, and implementing an LRU-based pruning strategy to stay within the maximum process limit. The manager also allocates unique process IDs, streams output to event logs, and cleans up resources when processes exit or are manually terminated.

This module formats shell command execution results into a structured XML-like record that gets added to the conversation history. It captures the executed command, exit code, duration, and output, then wraps it in `<user_shell_command>` tags so the model can reference what actually happened when a tool was invoked. The module essentially bridges executed shell commands with the protocol's response message format.

This utility module provides common helper functions and a feedback macro for the Codex core system. It includes exponential backoff logic for retries, error message parsing from JSON responses, path resolution, thread name normalization, and a command builder for resuming conversations by thread name or ID. The `feedback_tags!` macro enables structured logging of metadata that integrates with Codex's feedback collection system.

This module formats web search actions into human-readable detail strings for display purposes. It handles multiple types of search operations—including general searches (with query text truncation for multiple queries), page navigation, and in-page pattern finding—and falls back to a default query string when action details are unavailable or empty.

This file manages Windows sandbox configuration and setup for Codex, handling both modern and legacy sandbox modes (elevated vs. restricted token). It provides configuration resolution logic that prioritizes profile-level settings over global settings, bridges legacy feature flags to new sandbox mode configurations, and wraps platform-specific sandbox initialization functions with cross-platform stubs for non-Windows systems.

This file serves as the entry point for the integration test suite, aggregating all test modules from the `tests/all/` directory into a single test binary. It allows Rust to compile all integration tests together while keeping the actual test code organized in separate files.

This file provides a shared test utility library for the Codex test suite. It initializes deterministic test conditions (like process IDs), offers helpers for cross-platform path handling and configuration setup, and includes utilities for working with SSE event fixtures, waiting for async events with timeouts, and filesystem monitoring. The file also exports macros for conditionally skipping tests based on sandbox or platform constraints.

This test utility module provides helpers for managing child processes during integration tests. It offers functions to poll for a process ID from a file, check if a process is still running using the `kill -0` signal, and wait for a process to exit with a 2-second timeout. These primitives enable tests to coordinate with spawned subprocesses and verify their lifecycle.

# responses.rs

This file provides test infrastructure for mocking and validating HTTP requests and WebSocket connections to the OpenAI Responses API. It defines mock servers, request capture utilities, and convenience functions for constructing SSE (Server-Sent Events) responses with various payload types (messages, function calls, tool calls, reasoning, etc.). The file also enforces invariants on request bodies—ensuring tool call outputs are properly matched to their corresponding calls—to catch regressions early in test execution.

This file provides a test utility for simulating a Server-Sent Events (SSE) HTTP server with fine-grained control over chunk delivery timing. It supports two endpoints: `GET /v1/models` (returns an empty models list) and `POST /v1/responses` (streams SSE chunks), where each chunk can be gated behind an optional signal to test ordered, asynchronous streaming behavior. The server tracks incoming requests and completion timestamps, allowing tests to verify streaming correctness, proper ordering, and client-server interaction patterns in a controlled environment.

This file provides a test framework for the Codex system, enabling integration tests to create isolated, configurable instances of Codex with mock servers. It defines `TestCodexBuilder` for flexibly constructing test environments with custom configurations, authentication, and hooks, and `TestCodexHarness` as a convenience wrapper that manages both the mock server and test instance. The utilities allow tests to submit prompts, capture server requests, and inspect tool outputs (function calls, custom tools, apply patches) to verify system behavior end-to-end.

This file provides a test fixture builder for integration tests of the `codex-exec` command-line tool. It creates isolated temporary directories for home and working directory environments, configures the executable with necessary API credentials and optional mock server endpoints, and exposes paths for test assertions. The builder pattern allows tests to easily spawn configured instances of `codex-exec` with controlled environments.

This integration test file validates that the Codex system correctly includes and propagates HTTP headers in API requests to model providers. It tests three key scenarios: that subagent identifiers are properly passed via `x-openai-subagent` headers (for both "review" and custom task types), that model configuration overrides like reasoning summaries are respected in request payloads, and that workspace metadata (git commit hashes and remote URLs) are included in turn metadata headers during end-to-end workflows. The tests use a mock HTTP server to intercept and verify the headers and request bodies sent by the `ModelClient`.

This file contains integration tests that verify the task interruption workflow in Codex. It tests three scenarios: that interrupting a long-running tool command emits a `TurnAborted` event, that the system properly records tool execution history and synthesizes an abort message when resuming after an interrupt, and that a `<turn_aborted>` marker persists in the conversation history for the next model request. These tests ensure the interrupt mechanism cleanly halts in-progress operations and maintains coherent conversation state across interruptions.

This test suite validates the WebSocket communication layer for the Codex agent, ensuring it correctly establishes connections, sends/receives messages, and handles shell command execution through WebSocket streams. It covers critical scenarios including multi-turn interactions, preconnection handshakes, timing synchronization with delayed connections, and version-specific protocol behavior (v2 API with beta headers).

This file is an integration test suite for the `apply_patch` CLI tool, which applies code patches to files in a workspace. It tests the tool's core functionality—creating, modifying, moving, and deleting files via structured patch syntax—as well as error handling (invalid patches, missing files, path traversal attacks) and output modes (freeform, function calls, shell commands). The tests also verify that patches are properly validated before execution, that failed patches don't modify the filesystem, and that file changes are reported back to users via unified diffs.

This file is a comprehensive test suite for user approval workflows in Codex, validating how the system handles sandbox policies, approval policies, and various action types (file writes, network requests, command execution, and patch application). It defines a large matrix of test scenarios that verify the correct approval prompts are triggered based on policy combinations, and that approved/denied actions execute or block appropriately. The tests ensure security boundaries are enforced across different sandbox configurations (read-only, workspace-write, danger-full-access) and approval settings (never, on-request, unless-trusted, on-failure).

This test suite validates the token refresh lifecycle for the AuthManager, covering scenarios like successful refresh, staleness detection, error handling (both permanent and transient failures), and unauthorized recovery flows. It uses mocked OAuth endpoints to verify that tokens are properly updated in storage, cached appropriately, and that the system correctly distinguishes between recoverable and non-recoverable authentication failures.

This file contains integration and end-to-end tests for the Codex CLI's streaming functionality. It verifies that the CLI correctly handles streaming responses from the Responses API (both via mock servers and offline SSE fixtures), properly applies configuration overrides like custom model instructions, and accurately persists session data to disk with proper directory structure and JSON line formatting. The tests also validate that git repository information (branch, commit hash, remote URL) is correctly collected and included in session metadata.

This file contains integration tests for the Codex client's API request handling and response processing. It verifies that the client correctly constructs HTTP requests to the model API (including proper headers, authentication, message ordering, and configuration parameters), handles various authentication modes (API keys, ChatGPT tokens, environment variables), and properly manages conversation history across multiple turns. The tests use mock servers to validate request structure without making actual network calls, ensuring features like user instructions, developer instructions, reasoning effort settings, verbosity levels, rate limit tracking, and conversation resumption work as expected.

This file contains a comprehensive test suite for WebSocket-based communication in the Codex system, verifying that the client correctly sends requests, handles responses, manages connection reuse, and emits telemetry events through the Responses API. It tests both v1 and v2 versions of the WebSocket protocol, including scenarios like connection prewarming, request appending vs. creation based on message history, rate limiting, timing metrics, error handling, and various header management. The tests use a mock WebSocket server to simulate real API behavior without requiring actual network calls.

This test suite validates the delegation pattern in Codex, specifically how a parent agent forwards approval requests from sub-agents and processes their responses. The tests verify that execution approvals (for commands) and patch approvals (for file modifications) properly bubble up through the delegate, allow the parent to make decisions, and enable the sub-agent to proceed accordingly. A third test ensures the system correctly handles legacy reasoning delta events without breaking the newer event model.

This test suite validates how the Codex system handles collaboration mode instructions—custom developer guidelines that get passed to the AI model during interactions. It verifies that instructions are correctly injected into API requests, properly overridden when settings change, and intelligently deduplicated when unchanged, including edge cases like session resumption and empty instructions.

# compact.rs

This is a comprehensive test suite for the context compaction feature, which automatically or manually summarizes conversation history when it grows too large. The tests verify that summaries are correctly generated, injected into requests with proper formatting, persisted to rollout logs, and that the system handles edge cases like token limit overflows, model switching, session resumption, and retry logic after API errors. It also ensures compaction respects configuration limits, counts encrypted reasoning content accurately, and emits the appropriate lifecycle events and warnings to users.

# Summary

This file contains integration tests for the remote context compaction feature, which allows the Codex agent to compress conversation history when it grows too large. The tests verify that compaction correctly replaces old conversation turns with a compressed summary, can be triggered manually or automatically when token limits are exceeded, trims function call history to fit the context window, handles failures gracefully, and properly refreshes stale developer instructions after compaction. The tests also confirm that compacted history persists across session resumptions and that the compaction process sends appropriate requests to a remote service.

This file contains integration tests that verify the behavior of conversation compaction, resumption, and forking operations in Codex. Specifically, it validates that when conversations are compacted (summarized), resumed from a saved state, or forked at earlier points, the model-visible message history remains consistent and correctly structured across all these state transitions. The tests mock API responses and inspect the actual request payloads sent to the language model to ensure the conversation context is properly preserved or reconstructed.

This test suite validates that the Codex system correctly emits deprecation notices when users rely on legacy configuration options or feature flags. It tests four scenarios: using the old `use_experimental_unified_exec_tool` flag, the obsolete `experimental_instructions_file` setting, and deprecated web search feature flag variants. Each test confirms that the appropriate warning event is generated with accurate summary and detail messages to guide users toward the modern configuration approach.

This file contains integration tests for the command execution sandbox on macOS, specifically validating that the Seatbelt sandboxing mechanism properly enforces read-only file access policies. The tests verify expected behaviors like successful command execution, output truncation, proper error codes, and most importantly, that write attempts are blocked by the sandbox rather than succeeding. These tests ensure the security boundaries of the execution environment are working correctly before code is allowed to run.

This test suite validates the execution policy system that controls which shell and exec commands are allowed to run. It verifies that policy rules are correctly enforced to block forbidden commands, and tests edge cases like empty or whitespace-only scripts with collaboration mode enabled to ensure the system handles them gracefully without panicking or triggering internal invariant failures.

This integration test verifies that the thread forking mechanism correctly truncates conversation history at specified points. It creates a base conversation with three user messages, then forks the thread twice at different positions (after the second message, then after the first message), validating that each fork's rollout file contains only the expected conversation items up to the fork point. The test ensures that nested forks properly compute their history relative to their parent fork, not the original conversation.

This file contains integration tests for the `grep_files` tool, which allows the Codex agent to search for patterns in files using ripgrep. The tests verify that the tool correctly collects matching files while respecting include filters (e.g., only `.rs` files) and properly reports when no matches are found. The tests are Unix-only and require both network access and ripgrep to be installed.

This test suite validates the "hierarchical agents" feature, which automatically injects AGENTS.md file instructions into user prompts sent to the LLM. It verifies two scenarios: that existing AGENTS.md content is preserved and appended with standardized guidance about agent discovery, and that the feature generates appropriate instructions even when no local AGENTS.md file exists.

This file contains integration tests that verify image handling in the Codex system persists correctly to the rollout (request history) with the proper structure. It tests two user workflows—pasting a local image file and drag-dropping a base64-encoded image—ensuring that in both cases the resulting user message in the rollout contains the expected combination of text, image tags, and image data. The tests exist to prevent regressions in how multimodal user input is serialized and stored.

This file contains integration tests for the Codex core system's item lifecycle and event emission. It verifies that different types of conversation items (user messages, agent messages, reasoning blocks, web searches, and plans) are correctly created, streamed, and emitted as events throughout their lifecycle, including handling of content deltas and mode-specific behaviors like plan extraction in plan mode.

This test file validates that Codex correctly handles JSON schema-constrained responses from language models. It verifies that when a user provides a JSON schema requirement, the system properly sends that schema to the API with strict validation enabled, receives a JSON-formatted response, and parses it correctly without errors.

This file contains integration tests for a `list_dir` tool that allows an AI agent to list directory contents with configurable depth and pagination. The tests verify that the tool correctly returns entries at different recursion depths (shallow vs. deep directory traversals) and respects offset/limit parameters for pagination. All tests are currently disabled pending activation of the `list_dir` tool feature.

This file contains optional integration tests that verify the codex-rs CLI works end-to-end by making real requests to the OpenAI API. The tests are ignored by default to keep CI fast and deterministic, but developers can run them locally with a valid `OPENAI_API_KEY` to confirm the binary correctly handles streaming output and executes model-generated commands. The `run_live` helper spawns the CLI process while simultaneously capturing output to a buffer and streaming it to the terminal, allowing interactive observation of real API responses.

This integration test validates that the Codex system properly detects and reloads skill definitions when they change on disk. It creates a skill file, submits a turn that uses it, then modifies the skill and verifies that the updated version is sent to the API on the next turn—either through file watcher notifications or explicit cache clearing, ensuring skills remain current even when edited during a session.

This file serves as the test suite aggregator and initialization module for Codex's integration tests. It sets up a isolated test environment by creating a temporary `CODEX_HOME` directory and configuring argument dispatching before any tests run, ensuring tests can safely simulate Codex behavior without mutating the developer's actual system state. The file then imports all individual integration test modules, centralizing their organization and shared setup.

This test file validates that the ModelsManager correctly applies configuration overrides when retrieving model information. Specifically, it ensures that when a `tool_output_token_limit` is configured, it overrides the default truncation policy for models, while models without such overrides retain their standard truncation settings.

This test suite validates that turn-level model overrides (like switching to model "o3" with specific reasoning effort) are temporary and don't persist to or create the persistent `config.toml` file. It ensures the override mechanism respects the distinction between session-scoped parameter changes and permanent configuration, preventing accidental pollution of user configuration files.

This test suite validates the system's behavior when users switch between different AI models during a conversation. It ensures that model transitions are handled correctly in four key scenarios: appending model-switch context instructions to the next request, prioritizing model changes over personality changes in the same turn, stripping unsupported content types (like images) when switching to a text-only model, and updating the token context window calculations when switching to a model with a different context size. These tests prevent regressions in the model-switching logic that affects conversation history, content filtering, and token accounting.

This file tests that different AI models are configured with the correct set of tools available to them. It verifies model-specific tool selections by submitting a test request and asserting that each model receives its expected tool set, accounting for variations like platform differences (Windows vs Unix) and model-specific capabilities like the `apply_patch` tool for certain model versions.

This test suite validates the models cache time-to-live (TTL) and versioning behavior in Codex. It ensures that cached model lists are properly refreshed based on ETags, client version matching, and staleness, while avoiding unnecessary remote fetches when cache is still valid. The tests cover scenarios like renewing cache TTL on matching ETags, using cache when versions match, and refreshing when versions are missing or differ.

This test verifies that Codex correctly handles ETag-based cache invalidation for the models catalog. It ensures that when the server sends a new `X-Models-Etag` header in API responses, Codex refreshes the models list exactly once, and then avoids redundant refetches when receiving the same ETag again on subsequent requests.

This file is a comprehensive test suite for OpenTelemetry (OTEL) tracing and logging in the Codex system. It verifies that the system correctly emits observability events (traces, spans, and structured logs) at key points in the request-response lifecycle, including API calls, SSE (Server-Sent Events) processing, tool execution, and user approval decisions. The tests ensure that telemetry data captures relevant context like token counts, error messages, tool names, and approval sources, which is essential for monitoring, debugging, and auditing the system's behavior in production.

This test suite validates that the `OverrideTurnContext` operation does not prematurely record configuration changes (approval policies, environment variables, collaboration settings) to the rollout log when invoked without a subsequent user turn. It ensures that overrides are only persisted to the audit trail when they're actually applied in the context of a new user interaction.

This test verifies that when a user submits input during an ongoing streaming response, the Codex agent correctly handles the pending input by including it in a follow-up request. Specifically, it validates that the first request contains only the initial prompt, while a subsequent request triggered by the second user input includes both the original and new prompts in the conversation history.

This test suite validates that permission instruction messages are correctly included in API requests sent to the language model under various conditions. The tests verify that permissions are sent once on initial startup, added when approval policies change, not duplicated when policies remain unchanged, properly replayed when resuming sessions, accumulated correctly when forking threads, and accurately reflect sandbox configuration like writable directories. Essentially, it ensures the system communicates security and permission constraints to the model in a consistent and non-redundant manner across different session lifecycle scenarios.

This file contains integration tests for the personality feature in Codex, which allows customizing the AI assistant's communication style. The tests verify that personality templates are correctly applied to model instructions, that personality changes generate appropriate update messages in conversations, that the feature respects configuration and feature flags, and that both local and remote model personalities work as expected. The tests ensure personality overrides only add context when the personality actually changes and that disabling the feature prevents any personality customization from taking effect.

This test suite validates a personality migration system that automatically assigns a default personality setting to user configurations. It tests various scenarios including when migrations should be applied (presence of sessions with user activity), when they should be skipped (marker file exists, explicit personality already set, or no sessions), and edge cases like profile resolution errors and idempotency on repeated runs.

This test suite validates prompt caching behavior in the Codex system, specifically ensuring that cached prompt prefixes remain stable and consistent across multiple API requests while allowing turn-specific overrides to be applied without breaking cache efficiency. The tests verify that environment context, instructions, tools, and permissions messages are properly prefixed once and reused, that the prompt cache key remains constant even when turn parameters change, and that per-turn overrides (like model or sandbox policy changes) correctly emit updated messages while preserving the cacheable prefix for optimal token reuse.

This integration test verifies that when the OpenAI API returns a quota-exceeded error response, the Codex system properly translates it into exactly one user-facing error event rather than multiple or malformed events. It mocks an API server, submits a user input, and asserts that a single error event with the correct message is emitted before the turn completes.

This is a disabled integration test that validates the `read_file` tool functionality in the Codex agent. It verifies that when the agent calls `read_file` with a file path, offset, and limit, it correctly returns the requested lines of the file with proper line number formatting. The test is currently ignored and only runs on non-Windows systems, suggesting the feature is under development.

This file contains integration tests for the remote models feature, which allows Codex to fetch and use AI model definitions from a remote server rather than relying solely on bundled models. The tests verify that remote models are correctly merged with built-in models, properly prioritized, can override bundled models, respect visibility settings, apply their custom base instructions, and handle network timeouts gracefully. It also ensures that features like unified execution, truncation policies, and longest-prefix matching for model selection work correctly with remote models.

This test suite validates the request compression feature, ensuring that when enabled, the Codex backend compresses request bodies using zstd compression for ChatGPT authentication while explicitly avoiding compression for API-key authentication. The tests verify both that compression headers are set correctly and that the compressed payloads decompress to valid JSON.

This test suite validates the `request_user_input` feature, which allows the AI to prompt users for interactive approval or decisions during code generation sessions. It verifies that user input requests are properly captured, routed through the system, and that responses are correctly submitted back—while also ensuring the feature is appropriately gated and rejected in collaboration modes where it shouldn't be available.

This test suite validates the session resume functionality in Codex, ensuring that when a conversation is paused and later resumed, the system correctly preserves conversation history, handles model switches, and maintains system instructions. The tests cover scenarios including resuming with standard messages, resuming with reasoning events, switching models while preserving base instructions, and preventing duplicate model-switch notifications during resume operations.

This test verifies that the system emits a warning event when resuming a conversation thread where the AI model has changed between the original session and the resumed session. It constructs a scenario with a previous rollout saved under one model, attempts to resume it under a different current model, and asserts that an appropriate warning message is generated containing both model names.

# review.rs

Integration tests for the code review feature of Codex. These tests verify that submitting a `Review` operation correctly spawns an isolated child task, handles both structured JSON and plain-text review outputs from the model, filters out unwanted streaming events during review mode, respects custom review model configuration, and properly isolates review input from parent session history. The tests also ensure review results surface back into the parent conversation for later reference and that the review flow correctly resolves file paths using the current working directory context.

This file contains integration tests for the Codex remote MCP (Model Context Protocol) client. It validates end-to-end functionality of MCP tool invocation across different transport mechanisms (stdio and HTTP), including proper handling of environment variables, image responses, and OAuth authentication. The tests verify that MCP servers can be spawned, tools can be discovered and called, and results are correctly returned to the AI model and formatted appropriately based on the model's capabilities.

This is an integration test suite for Codex's thread/rollout discovery system. It validates that the codebase can locate conversation session files by various criteria (ID, name, archived status) and that the lookup logic correctly prioritizes database records over filesystem scans while gracefully falling back when needed. The tests also verify the system handles edge cases like gitignore rules and works with real rollout files produced by the recorder.

This test suite validates the search tool feature, which allows users to dynamically discover and activate MCP (Model Context Protocol) tools through a BM25 search interface. The tests verify that the search tool is properly exposed when enabled, includes appropriate developer instructions, hides MCP tools until explicitly searched for, maintains search selections within a single conversation turn while resetting them between turns, and correctly unions results from multiple searches within the same turn.

This file contains macOS-specific tests for Seatbelt sandbox enforcement, validating that file access restrictions are properly applied based on different sandbox policies. It tests critical scenarios like protecting `.git` folders when a repo is the writable root, allowing full access under danger mode, and ensuring system tools like `openpty` and `java_home` function correctly within the sandbox.

This test suite validates the shell command execution feature in Codex by simulating various execution scenarios. It tests core functionality including basic command execution, login shell handling, multi-line and piped output, timeout behavior, and Unicode character support across different platforms. The tests mock API responses and verify that commands execute correctly and produce properly formatted output with exit codes and timing information.

This file tests how shell command output is serialized and formatted in different scenarios. It validates that output is presented as structured plain text (with exit code, timing, and content) when the apply_patch tool is enabled, versus unstructured JSON when disabled, and verifies that truncation, error handling, and file operations work correctly across different shell execution modes (Shell, ShellCommand, LocalShell).

This file contains integration tests for the shell snapshot feature, which captures shell environment state (aliases, exports, PATH settings) before executing commands. The tests verify that both the unified exec tool and shell_command functions correctly generate snapshots, pass them to command execution, and clean them up on shutdown, across different operating systems (Linux, macOS, Windows). It ensures snapshots work alongside other tools like apply_patch and validates the correct command structure is used when invoking snapshots.

This test suite validates the skills system in Codex, which allows users to extend the AI assistant's capabilities with custom instructions and predefined workflows. The tests verify three key behaviors: that user-provided skills are correctly included in prompts sent to the language model, that malformed skill files are properly caught and reported as errors rather than silently failing, and that system-provided skills (embedded with the application) are automatically installed and discoverable alongside user skills.

This test suite validates the SQLite state database functionality for the Codex system. It ensures that thread metadata, rollout files, user messages, and tool call logs are correctly persisted to and retrieved from the state database, including scenarios where existing rollout files need to be backfilled into a newly initialized database.

This integration test verifies that the Codex system can recover and accept new user input after a streaming API request fails. It simulates a scenario where an initial request returns a 500 error, confirms the system properly emits an Error event and releases its state with a TurnComplete event, then validates that a subsequent follow-up request succeeds without being rejected or deadlocked. The test ensures stream error handling doesn't leave the system in a broken state.

This test verifies that the Codex agent properly handles incomplete Server-Sent Events (SSE) streams by retrying the request. It simulates a scenario where the first API response closes prematurely without sending a `response.completed` event, then validates that the agent automatically retries and eventually succeeds when the second attempt returns a complete stream with all expected events.

This integration test validates the text encoding detection and conversion logic that handles shell output from Windows/WSL environments, where legacy code pages (CP1251, CP866, Windows-1252, Latin-1) may be used instead of UTF-8. The tests ensure that the `StreamOutput::from_utf8_lossy()` method correctly identifies and decodes various legacy encodings—particularly Cyrillic and special punctuation—while falling back gracefully to lossy UTF-8 conversion when detection fails, addressing issue #6178.

This test suite validates the tool execution harness for Codex, verifying that AI-invoked tools (shell commands, plan updates, and patch application) execute correctly and emit appropriate events. It uses a mock server to simulate API responses and checks both the tool outputs and internal event streams to ensure tools behave as expected, including proper error handling for malformed inputs.

This test suite validates that Codex can execute multiple tools in parallel rather than sequentially. It contains integration tests that mock LLM responses containing multiple tool calls, measure execution timing, and verify that tools like custom sync tools and shell commands run concurrently. The tests also verify that tool results are properly grouped in the protocol messages and that tools start executing before the LLM finishes streaming their definitions, enabling true parallelism during tool invocation.

This file contains integration tests for the Codex tool execution system, specifically validating how the system handles shell commands, custom tools, sandboxing policies, and timeouts. The tests verify critical behaviors like permission escalation rejection, sandbox enforcement, tool availability based on feature flags, timeout handling with background processes, and error message truncation for spawn failures. Each test simulates realistic tool-use scenarios by mocking API responses and inspecting the resulting outputs to ensure the system behaves correctly under various conditions.

This file contains integration tests for tool output truncation in the Codex system. It verifies that when tool outputs (from shell commands and Model Context Protocol servers) exceed configured token or character limits, they are properly truncated before being sent back to the language model, with appropriate truncation markers inserted. The tests also ensure that truncation doesn't occur when the configured limits are generous enough, and that different truncation policies (token-based vs. byte-based) report the correct type of metric in their summary messages.

This test suite validates that turn state headers are correctly managed across multiple conversational turns in both HTTP and WebSocket connections. The tests verify that when a server provides a turn state value in a response, the client persists and echoes that state in subsequent requests within the same turn, then clears it when starting a new turn—ensuring proper state isolation between conversation exchanges.

This file contains integration tests for the undo functionality in Codex, verifying that the system can correctly revert file changes made during AI-assisted turns. The tests use git snapshots (ghost commits) to restore files to their previous state, covering scenarios like removing newly-created files, restoring edits to tracked and untracked files, handling file moves/renames, and ensuring undo only reverts the latest turn while preserving unrelated user changes. Each test sets up a git repository, runs an AI turn that applies patches, then invokes undo and asserts the expected file state changes.

# Summary

This is a comprehensive integration test suite for the Unified Exec feature in Codex, which provides a unified interface for executing commands and managing interactive terminal sessions. The tests verify the full lifecycle of command execution—from initialization through output streaming to session termination—covering scenarios like TTY allocation, stdin interaction, process reuse, output truncation, sandbox isolation, and background process management. Key test scenarios include intercepting apply-patch operations, emitting begin/end events, handling delayed output, respecting workdir overrides, and ensuring long-running sessions survive turn boundaries while being cleanly terminated on interrupt.

This test suite verifies that the system properly warns users when unstable/under-development features are enabled in their configuration. It validates both that warnings are emitted with appropriate messaging when features like `child_agents_md` are activated, and that users can suppress these warnings via a configuration flag. The tests ensure the warning system works as a user-facing safety mechanism to inform developers about experimental functionality.

This is an integration test that verifies the user notification system works correctly on Unix-like systems. It tests that when a user submits input to Codex and receives a response, a configured notification script is invoked with the correct payload (including the input message and assistant's response). The test sets up a mock server, creates a temporary notification script, submits a request through Codex, and validates that the notification script receives the expected JSON data about the completed turn.

This test suite validates the user shell command execution feature in the Codex system. It verifies that users can execute arbitrary shell commands (like `ls` and `cat`) in a controlled environment, with proper handling of command output, interruption, and integration with the model's active turn. The tests also ensure shell command history is persisted, shared with the AI model for context, and that large outputs are truncated to stay within token limits without being truncated multiple times.

This integration test suite validates the `view_image` tool and local image attachment functionality in Codex. The tests verify that images are properly encoded, resized to acceptable dimensions (≤2048×768px), and sent to the API as base64 data URLs, while also ensuring proper error handling for edge cases like missing files, directories, unsupported file types, and text-only models. One additional test confirms that invalid images received from the API are replaced with a placeholder message on retry.

This test suite validates the web search functionality in Codex, specifically ensuring that the `WebSearchMode` configuration correctly controls whether web searches use cached results or live external data. The tests verify that the mode setting properly overrides legacy feature flags, respects sandbox policy constraints (cached for read-only, live for full access), and persists correctly across conversation turns. It appears to be guarding against regressions in a feature migration from older flag-based controls to the newer `WebSearchMode` system.

This test suite validates the WebSocket-to-HTTP fallback mechanism in the Codex client. It verifies that when WebSocket connections fail—either due to upgrade requirement errors or exhausted retries—the client gracefully switches to HTTP transport and that this fallback decision persists across subsequent conversation turns. The tests ensure the fallback logic respects retry budgets and doesn't waste attempts retrying a failing transport before switching.

This file implements a client for communicating with the Codex app server via JSON-RPC protocol. It handles spawning the server process, sending requests (thread management, user input), reading responses, and managing client state like the current thread ID. The client automatically declines approval requests for command execution and file changes, making it suitable for automated or debug scenarios.

This file defines the input parsing layer for the debug client's interactive command interface. It distinguishes between user messages (sent directly) and meta-commands (prefixed with `:`) like creating threads, resuming conversations, and requesting help, then parses raw input strings into typed commands with validation for required arguments.

This is a minimal interactive CLI client for the Codex app server that allows users to start, resume, and switch between conversation threads. It spawns a background connection to the Codex server, handles user input for sending messages and managing threads, and displays server responses in real-time. The tool supports configuration options like approval policies, model selection, and auto-approval of actions.

This module manages formatted console output for a debug client, handling the display of server/client messages and an interactive prompt while preventing output interleaving. It provides thread-safe output operations that respect terminal capabilities (color support, TTY detection) and the `NO_COLOR` environment variable, while maintaining a persistent prompt that gets cleared and redrawn around other output to keep the interactive experience clean.

This file implements a message reader that runs in a background thread, listening to JSON-RPC messages from a server process. It parses incoming requests, responses, and notifications—automatically approving or declining command and file-change requests based on configuration—and routes responses back to application state while emitting formatted output for user visibility. It's the primary communication bridge between the debug client and the server, handling both protocol-level message handling and user-facing output formatting.

Defines the client-side state management structures for the debug client, tracking in-flight requests by ID, the current thread context, and known threads, along with enums representing the types of pending operations and events that can be received from the server.

This file provides a Unix-specific entry point for an executable wrapper around the `execve` system call. On non-Unix platforms, it fails with a clear error message, while on Unix systems it delegates to the actual implementation in the `codex_exec_server` library. It exists to abstract away platform-specific initialization so the main binary can be cross-platform compatible.

This file serves as the entry point for the Codex execution server's MCP (Model Context Protocol) implementation, with a Unix-only requirement. It conditionally exports the actual server logic from the library for Unix systems while rejecting non-Unix platforms with a clear error message.

This file serves as the platform-specific entry point for the execution server, exposing the Unix/POSIX implementation of code execution and MCP (Model Context Protocol) server functionality. It conditionally compiles the `posix` module on Unix systems and re-exports its key components—the main execution wrapper, server initialization, and result types—to make them available to the rest of the codebase.

This file implements a sandboxed shell execution server with fine-grained privilege escalation for the Codex MCP. It spawns a patched Bash process that intercepts every `exec()` call through a wrapper program, sends those calls back to the MCP server for policy evaluation, and can escalate privileges on a per-command basis if the policy permits it. The file defines the main entry points for both the MCP server and the execve wrapper, the core policy evaluation logic that determines whether commands should be allowed, prompted, or forbidden, and the infrastructure for loading execution policies from the Codex configuration system.

This file implements the client-side logic for privilege escalation on POSIX systems. It communicates with an escalation daemon via a socket to request execution of a command, then either escalates privileges by forwarding I/O file descriptors to the daemon, runs the command directly via `execv`, or returns a denial error depending on the daemon's response. The escalation protocol allows the exec server to transparently handle privilege-requiring operations by delegating them to a privileged process.

This file defines the message protocol for a privilege escalation mechanism in the exec-server, enabling controlled interception and delegation of exec() calls between a patched bash client and a server. It specifies the data structures and environment variables used to request execution of a command (with file path, arguments, working directory, and environment), receive authorization decisions (Run, Escalate, or Deny), forward file descriptors, and report exit codes.

This file implements a server that mediates privilege escalation requests on POSIX systems by enforcing an escalation policy before allowing elevated command execution. It receives escalation requests through Unix sockets, consults a pluggable policy to determine whether to allow, deny, or escalate each command, and then either executes the command with elevated privileges or returns a deny response. The server integrates with Codex's sandboxing system to run user commands in a controlled environment while handling privileged operations through a separate escalation channel.

Defines the trait interface that determines whether and how to escalate execve requests from clients in the POSIX execution server. This allows different escalation policies to be plugged in to make authorization decisions based on the target file, arguments, and working directory.

This file implements an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server that exposes shell command execution as a tool. It wraps the underlying escalate server to execute bash commands with sandboxing, policy enforcement, and timeout management, while also exposing a custom method to update sandbox state configuration. The server communicates over stdio and is designed to be controlled by an AI model or other MCP client.

This file implements a security policy evaluator that decides whether to allow, prompt the user about, or deny process execution requests. It bridges the execution policy rules with the MCP (Model Context Protocol) messaging system, allowing it to ask users for permission via interactive prompts when a command's execution status is uncertain, and enforces sandbox permission constraints based on the policy outcome.

This file implements async Unix domain socket communication with support for passing file descriptors between processes. It provides two main abstractions: `AsyncSocket` for stream-based messaging (with length-prefixed frames and JSON serialization) and `AsyncDatagramSocket` for datagram-based messaging, both capable of sending and receiving file descriptors via POSIX control messages. The implementation handles the low-level details of `sendmsg`/`recvmsg` syscalls, buffer management, and async I/O integration with Tokio.

This file implements a `Stopwatch` utility that tracks elapsed time while supporting pause/resume semantics, primarily for enforcing execution timeouts in the exec-server. It generates a cancellation token that fires after a specified duration, but the elapsed time only advances when the stopwatch is running—pauses (often used to wait for I/O or external operations) don't count toward the limit. Nested pauses are reference-counted so the timer only resumes when all pauses are lifted, allowing multiple concurrent operations to independently pause the clock.
This file serves as the entry point for the integration test suite, aggregating all test modules located in the `tests/suite/` directory into a single test binary for execution.

This is a test utilities library that sets up integration test infrastructure for the execution server. It provides helper functions to create an MCP (Model Context Protocol) transport connected to the codex execution service, configure sandbox policies (read-only or writable), and simulate a test client that can accept or reject elicitation requests. The code essentially bootstraps a full end-to-end test environment by launching the actual execution server as a subprocess and managing the communication protocol with it.

This integration test verifies the elicitation workflow when executing privileged commands in a read-only sandbox. It sets up an MCP server with an execution policy that requires user approval for `git init` commands, then confirms that the proper elicitation message is sent, auto-approved, and the command executes successfully outside the sandbox with elevated privileges.

This test verifies that the MCP (Model Context Protocol) server's `list_tools` endpoint correctly exposes a "shell" tool with the expected schema and metadata. It sets up a temporary server environment with a rules file, calls the list_tools RPC method, and asserts that the returned tool definition matches the expected structure including the command parameters (command, workdir, timeout_ms, login) and their JSON schema constraints.

This test module file conditionally includes integration tests for the exec-server, specifically enabling test suites for `accept_elicitation` and `list_tools` only on macOS (ARM64) and Linux platforms. It serves as a platform-gated test organization layer that prevents running certain tests on unsupported operating systems like Windows.

This file defines the command-line interface (CLI) argument parsing for the Codex executor. It specifies all available CLI flags, options, and subcommands (like `resume` and `review`), along with their validation rules and help text. The file also includes special handling for the `resume` command to reinterpret positional arguments based on flags, allowing flexible argument ordering when global flags appear before the prompt.

Defines the event processing abstraction layer for the Codex executor, establishing a trait that handles agent events and controls the execution lifecycle through a `CodexStatus` state machine. It provides utilities for displaying configuration summaries, processing individual events from the agent, and persisting the final agent message to disk, allowing different implementations to customize how the executor responds to agent activity.

This file implements a human-readable event processor for the Codex CLI that converts execution events into formatted terminal output. It handles all the events generated during an agent session—such as command execution, file changes, tool invocations, and reasoning steps—and displays them to stderr with appropriate styling (colors, bold text, etc.) controlled by ANSI escape codes. The processor respects color settings, supports optional features like showing agent reasoning, and produces a final output summary with token usage statistics.

This file implements an event processor that converts low-level protocol events from the Codex execution engine into a stream of structured thread events, which are then serialized to JSON and printed line-by-line (JSONL format). It tracks the lifecycle of various concurrent operations—like command executions, tool calls, file patches, and collaborative agent interactions—by maintaining state for in-progress items and emitting start/update/complete events as they progress. The processor also handles special cases like missing begin events and aggregates data like command output before emitting completion events.

This file defines the event schema for the Codex execution engine, establishing the JSONL event stream contract between the agent runtime and clients. It provides strongly-typed, serializable representations of all lifecycle events (thread start/stop, turn execution, item processing) and the various work items the agent can emit (commands, file changes, tool calls, web searches, reasoning). The types support both JSON serialization and TypeScript code generation, enabling consistent event handling across backend and frontend systems.

This file is the main entry point for the Codex CLI executor, orchestrating the initialization and execution of AI-driven code operations. It loads configuration, sets up logging and telemetry, manages thread lifecycle for long-running tasks, and routes user input (prompts or code review requests) through an event processing pipeline. The file handles multiple output modes (human-readable or JSON Lines), supports both cloud and local OSS models, enforces security policies (sandboxing, approvals), and gracefully manages async event streams with support for multi-threaded operations and interruption handling.

This file is the entry point for the `codex-exec` command-line binary. It uses argument dispatching to support dual functionality: when invoked normally, it launches the Codex agent with parsed CLI options; when invoked as `codex-linux-sandbox`, it delegates to sandboxed command execution logic using Landlock and seccomp. This design allows shipping two distinct executables' worth of functionality within a single binary.

Integration test entry point that aggregates all test suites for the execution module. It imports test modules from the `tests/suite/` directory and a specific test for event processor JSON output functionality, allowing the entire test suite to run as a single binary.

This file is a comprehensive test suite for `EventProcessorWithJsonOutput`, validating how various protocol events are transformed into a standardized thread event stream for JSON output serialization. It tests the processor's ability to correctly handle event lifecycles (begin/end pairs), aggregate state changes, assign consistent item identifiers, and convert low-level execution events (commands, tool calls, patches, plans) into high-level structured items that clients can consume.

This test suite validates that the `--add-dir` CLI flag works correctly in the Codex executor, ensuring the argument is properly wired and accepts multiple directory paths. The tests confirm both single and multiple directory additions execute successfully by invoking the CLI with temporary directories and verifying exit code 0.

This file contains integration tests for the `apply-patch` functionality in the `codex-exec` CLI tool. It verifies that the tool can parse patch files and apply file modifications (create, update, delete) both as a standalone command and when invoked through LLM tool calls, including support for both unified and freeform patch formats.

This test verifies that the Codex executor correctly reads and uses the API key from environment variables when making requests to the Codex API. It mocks an API server, executes a command with the executor, and asserts that the Authorization header contains the expected bearer token from the env var.

This test suite validates the ephemeral mode functionality of the Codex executor, ensuring that session data is persisted to disk by default but completely discarded when the `--ephemeral` flag is used. The tests run the CLI with a mocked SSE response fixture and verify the expected behavior by counting `.jsonl` session files in the home directory.

This test verifies that the Codex executor exits with a non-zero status code when a required MCP (Model Context Protocol) server fails to initialize. It sets up a broken MCP server configuration, attempts to run a command, and asserts that the process fails with an appropriate error message rather than proceeding with execution.

This file serves as a test suite aggregator that organizes integration tests into a modular structure. It imports multiple test modules covering different execution scenarios (patching, authentication, sandboxing, MCP integration, etc.), allowing the test suite to be maintained as separate, focused test files while being compiled and run together as a unified whole.

This test suite verifies that the `codex-exec` tool correctly sets and sends an "Originator" HTTP header to identify itself when communicating with the Codex server. It validates both the default originator behavior and the ability to override it via an environment variable, ensuring the client properly identifies its source in API requests.

This test verifies that when a user provides an output schema file via the `--output-schema` flag, the Codex executor correctly includes that schema in the API request sent to the model. It creates a sample JSON schema, runs the executor with that schema, and validates that the resulting request payload contains the schema properly formatted under a `text.format` field with the correct structure.

This test suite validates the session resume functionality of a Codex CLI tool. It ensures that users can resume previous conversations by latest session or by explicit session ID, and that resumed sessions correctly append new interactions to existing files while preserving configuration overrides, working directory filters, and attached images. The tests use unique markers and temporary directories to verify that resume operations target the correct session files and maintain conversation continuity.

This test suite validates that the sandbox implementation (using Landlock on Linux and Seatbelt on macOS) correctly isolates and restricts process execution. It includes capability probes to skip tests when sandbox enforcement is unavailable, and exercises critical functionality like multiprocessing locks, system calls (getpwuid), filesystem access boundaries, and Unix socket communication within sandboxed environments.

This test verifies that `codex-exec` properly exits with a non-zero status code when the OpenAI Responses API server reports an error, ensuring automation tools can detect and respond to failures appropriately. It mocks a server that immediately returns a `response.failed` event and confirms the CLI command exits with code 1 rather than succeeding silently.

This build script ensures that the Rust build system rebuilds the crate whenever the `src/default.policy` file changes, allowing policy configuration updates to be properly incorporated into the compiled binary without requiring manual cache invalidation.

This file defines `ArgMatcher`, an enum that describes what type of command-line argument should be accepted and validated in execution policies. It supports various argument kinds (literal strings, files, integers, sed commands, etc.) and provides methods to query cardinality constraints and map matchers to their corresponding argument types. The Starlark trait implementations allow `ArgMatcher` to be used as a value type within Starlark policy scripts.

This file implements argument resolution logic that matches observed command-line arguments against expected argument patterns. It handles three types of patterns—fixed positional arguments at the start, optional variable-length arguments in the middle, and fixed positional arguments at the end—validating that all provided arguments conform to the declared patterns and reporting errors when there are too few, too many, or mismatched arguments.

This file defines `ArgType`, an enumeration that classifies command-line arguments by their expected type or purpose—whether they're literal strings, file paths (readable or writable), positive integers, sed commands, or unknown types. It exists to enable validation and safety analysis of command arguments, particularly for the execution policy system that needs to determine if a command might read or write files. The `validate` method checks that actual argument values conform to their declared type, while `might_write_file` helps identify potentially dangerous operations.

Defines a comprehensive error type for the execution policy system, capturing all possible failures that can occur during argument parsing, validation, and file permission checking. The `Error` enum is serializable and provides structured, tagged error variants that distinguish between argument parsing issues (missing values, unknown options), specification problems (no spec for program, multiple vararg patterns), and file access violations (paths outside allowed folders). This enables consistent error handling and reporting across the codebase.

This file defines `ExecCall`, a data structure that represents a command execution request with a program name and its arguments. It provides serialization support and display formatting to render the command as a human-readable string, serving as a core abstraction for representing executable commands throughout the execution policy system.

This file implements a security checker that validates execution calls against a policy by verifying that file arguments reference only approved readable and writable directories. It takes a matched execution (program name and arguments), resolves relative paths to absolute paths using the current working directory, and ensures all file arguments stay within policy-defined folder boundaries before returning the resolved executable path.

This is the public interface library for a legacy execution policy enforcement system written in Rust. It exposes modules for parsing and validating command execution against security policies defined in a Starlark-based policy language, including argument matching, type checking, and execution validation. The library provides a default built-in policy and a parser to load custom policies for controlling which programs can execute and with what arguments.

This is a command-line tool that validates whether system commands are safe to execute according to a security policy. It loads a policy file (or a default one), evaluates an incoming command against that policy, and outputs a JSON result indicating if the command is safe, forbidden, or unverifiable. The tool supports two input modes: raw command arguments or JSON-encoded exec calls, and returns different exit codes to signal policy compliance to callers.

This file defines the `Opt` struct and `OptMeta` enum to represent command-line options in a Starlark-based execution policy system. `Opt` encapsulates a command-line flag (like `-h` or `--help`), its metadata about whether it takes a value, and whether it's required, while `OptMeta` distinguishes between flags that take no value versus those that take a typed argument. The implementations make these types compatible with the Starlark interpreter so they can be used and manipulated within policy scripts.

This file defines the core `Policy` struct that enforces execution rules by checking whether a given program execution is allowed. It validates exec calls against forbidden program patterns and substrings, then delegates to program-specific specs for detailed matching logic. It also provides methods to verify that positive and negative test examples in the policy are working as expected.

This file implements a Starlark-based policy parser that interprets declarative security policies for controlling program execution. It uses the Starlark language (a Python-like dialect) to allow users to define which programs can run, what arguments they accept, and what patterns are forbidden, then compiles these policy definitions into a `Policy` object that can be enforced at runtime. The `PolicyBuilder` accumulates program specifications and restrictions during Starlark evaluation, while the builtin functions (`define_program`, `forbid_substrings`, `forbid_program_regex`) provide the API that policy files call to specify their rules.

This file defines the core policy specification and validation engine for controlling program execution. `ProgramSpec` encapsulates allowed options, argument patterns, and forbidden rules for a program, then its `check()` method parses and validates actual command-line invocations against those rules—parsing flags and options, matching positional arguments to patterns, and ensuring required options are present. It also provides test harness methods (`verify_should_match_list`, `verify_should_not_match_list`) to validate that the policy specification correctly allows/denies expected command examples.

This file validates a narrow subset of sed commands to determine if they are safely executable. It specifically recognizes and approves line-range print commands (like `122,202p`), rejecting all other sed syntax as potentially unsafe, which appears to support an execution policy that restricts what sed operations are allowed to run.

This file defines the data structures representing a validated command execution that has been approved by a security policy. `ValidExec` encapsulates the program name, flags, options, and arguments that passed policy validation, along with an optional list of safer system paths to prefer over the program name in `$PATH`. The supporting types (`MatchedFlag`, `MatchedOpt`, `MatchedArg`) represent individual components of the command, each with validation logic and type information to ensure safety before execution.

This file serves as the entry point for the test suite, aggregating all integration tests defined in submodules located in the `tests/suite/` directory. It allows the test harness to run all tests through a single compiled binary rather than treating each test file independently.

This test file verifies that the default execution policy correctly rejects all items in its "bad list" (known-bad examples). It ensures that no negative examples unexpectedly pass the policy checks, catching regressions where unsafe code might be incorrectly allowed.

This file contains unit tests for the `cp` command execution policy, verifying that the policy correctly validates and matches `cp` invocations against expected argument patterns (readable source files and a writeable destination). The tests cover edge cases like missing arguments, insufficient arguments, and valid single and multiple file copy scenarios to ensure the policy enforcement mechanism works as intended.

This test file validates that all entries in the "good list" (approved/allowed items) pass the default execution policy without violations. It ensures the policy's whitelist is internally consistent and that no legitimate examples are incorrectly flagged as violations.

This file contains test cases for the `head` command's execution policy validation. It verifies that the legacy execpolicy library correctly accepts or rejects various `head` invocations—including cases with no arguments, single files, flag-option pairs, and invalid argument values—to ensure the security policy enforcement works as intended for this specific command.

This test file validates that the legacy execution policy system correctly enforces literal argument matching for subcommands. It verifies both the success case where arguments match the policy definition exactly and the failure case where an unexpected argument value is rejected with a descriptive error.

This test suite validates the `ls` command execution policy, ensuring the legacy execpolicy module correctly parses and validates `ls` invocations with various combinations of flags and file arguments.
