It maintains two history sources—persistent cross-session history and local submissions from the current session—and provides a state machine that decides when arrow keys should navigate history versus perform normal cursor movement. The `HistoryEntry` struct captures full draft state including text, mentions, images, and pending pastes so that recalled messages can be fully restored, while navigation logic enforces boundary checks to preserve usability of multiline text editing.

This file implements a filterable command popup that displays both built-in slash commands and user-defined custom prompts in the UI. It handles filtering based on partial text input (e.g., typing `/in` shows commands starting with "in"), ranking matches by exact matches first then prefix matches, and managing selection/navigation through the list. The popup respects feature flags to conditionally show/hide certain commands and automatically excludes user prompts that collide with built-in command names to avoid duplication.

This file implements a reusable multi-line text input dialog for collecting custom user prompts (such as review instructions). It handles rendering a titled input form with optional context information, manages keyboard input including submit (Enter) and cancel (Esc) actions, and invokes a callback when the user submits non-empty text. The view is designed as a bottom pane popup within the TUI application's layout system.

This file implements a UI view for toggling experimental features in the Codex TUI. It displays a scrollable list of feature flags with descriptions, allows users to enable/disable them via keyboard input (space to toggle, arrow keys to navigate), and persists changes to the config file when the view is closed. The view integrates with the app's event system to communicate feature flag updates.

This file implements a feedback submission UI overlay that collects optional user notes and uploads them along with logs and diagnostic data. It handles the final step of the feedback flow by rendering a text input area, managing keyboard input for submission or cancellation, and then uploading the feedback with appropriate follow-up instructions—either to an internal Slack channel for OpenAI employees or to a GitHub issue template for external users.

This file implements the visual state and rendering logic for a file-search popup in the TUI. It manages the query text, search results, and selection state, handling the distinction between pending queries (still searching) and displayed results (already arrived). The popup renders matching file paths with highlighted match indices and provides navigation methods to move selection up and down through the results.

This file renders the footer section of a chat composer UI, displaying contextual hints, shortcuts, and status information. It's responsible for translating `FooterProps` (high-level state like whether a task is running) into styled text lines, while keeping rendering logic separate from state management. The file includes sophisticated width-based fallback rules that intelligently hide or shorten footer hints (like "? for shortcuts" or "shift+tab to cycle") when terminal space is constrained, prioritizing certain content (like active queue messages) over others. It also handles transient UI states like quit confirmations and keyboard shortcut overlays, and manages conditional display of collaboration mode indicators and context window usage.

This file implements a generic list-based selection popup UI component with filtering, keyboard navigation, and customizable rendering. It manages both the data model (`SelectionItem`) and interactive state (`ListSelectionView`), handling search queries, disabled items, scrolling, and multi-column layout modes. The component supports keyboard shortcuts, number-key selection, and callbacks that fire when items are chosen, making it reusable across different selection dialogs in the Codex TUI.

# Summary

This file defines `BottomPane`, the interactive footer widget of the chat UI that manages the prompt input composer and a stack of modal views (popups/overlays). It handles layered input routing where active modals get first chance to consume keys like Ctrl+C before delegating to the composer, while delegating higher-level decisions like quit and interrupt to the parent `ChatWidget`. The pane also manages time-based UI elements like transient hints and task-running status indicators that need redraws even when idle.

This module provides a reusable multi-select picker widget for terminal UIs, enabling users to toggle multiple items on/off from a filtered list. It supports fuzzy search to quickly narrow down choices, optional item reordering via arrow keys, and customizable callbacks for state changes, confirmation, and cancellation. The widget is designed to fit into a larger TUI bottom pane system with configurable styling, preview display, and keyboard shortcuts.

This module detects rapid character input streams on platforms like Windows (which don't support bracketed paste) and classifies them as paste events rather than normal typing. It implements a state machine that temporarily holds the first fast character to avoid UI flicker, buffers subsequent rapid characters as a paste, keeps Enter treated as a newline during paste events, and retroactively captures already-inserted text when a paste is detected late. The state machine is timing-based with platform-specific thresholds, and it never mutates the UI directly—instead it returns decisions for the caller (`ChatComposer`) to apply.

This file defines shared constants and utilities for popup dialogs in the bottom pane UI. It establishes a maximum row limit for popup display consistency and provides a reusable footer hint line that instructs users how to confirm or dismiss popups using Enter and Escape keys.

This file handles parsing and expanding custom prompt commands in the Codex UI. It parses slash commands like `/prompts:name KEY=VALUE` to extract arguments and then expands template placeholders (both named like `$USER` and positional like `$1`) within saved prompt templates. The module carefully preserves text element metadata (such as ranges for inline images or special content) throughout tokenization and expansion, enabling rich formatting to survive argument parsing.

This widget renders a display of user messages that have been queued while a conversation turn is still in progress, showing them in a compact format with word-wrapping and a truncation indicator for messages longer than 3 lines. It provides visual feedback to users that their inputs are pending, along with a keyboard hint (`Alt+Up`) for editing the queued messages.

This file implements responsive layout logic for a user input dialog overlay in a terminal UI. It dynamically allocates screen space among multiple sections (progress bar, question text, options, notes, and footer) based on available height, gracefully collapsing or truncating elements as space becomes constrained. The layout adapts differently depending on whether options are present, prioritizing which UI elements to preserve or shrink in tight spaces.

# Request User Input Overlay

This module implements an interactive terminal UI for collecting structured user responses to a series of questions. Each question can have multiple-choice options, freeform text notes, or both, with focus switching between option selection and text input. The overlay manages answer state, validates completion, handles question navigation, and submits responses back to the application when finished or interrupted.

This file renders the "request user input" overlay UI—a form-like interface that presents survey questions one at a time, allowing users to select from options and optionally add notes before proceeding. It handles layout calculations to fit questions, multiple-choice options, notes fields, and footer hints into a constrained screen area, plus a special confirmation dialog that warns users about unanswered questions before submission.

This file defines a reusable scroll and selection state manager for vertical list menus in the TUI. It tracks which item is currently selected and maintains a scroll window to keep the selected item visible, supporting wrap-around navigation (moving up from the first item cycles to the last, and vice versa). The structure abstracts common list interaction patterns so they can be consistently applied across different menu components in the bottom pane.

This file provides shared rendering utilities for selection popup menus in the TUI, handling layout, text wrapping, and styling of list items. It defines `GenericDisplayRow` as a presentation-focused data structure for popup items (with name, description, disabled state, and optional visual markers like shortcuts and category tags), then implements flexible column-width strategies and multi-line text wrapping to support various popup styles—from dense single-line lists to wrapped multi-line descriptions with aligned columns. The core public functions (`render_rows`, `render_rows_stable_col_widths`, `measure_rows_height`, etc.) allow callers to render scrollable lists and pre-calculate required height, ensuring consistent behavior across overlays like model selection, approvals, and user input prompts.

This file implements a searchable popup menu for selecting and inserting "skills" (likely code snippets, commands, or similar reusable items) in the terminal UI. It handles filtering mentions by fuzzy-matching against display names and search terms, manages user selection via keyboard navigation, and renders the popup with matching results and keyboard hints. The popup tracks scroll state to show a limited number of results at once while allowing the user to navigate through all matches.

This file implements a terminal UI panel for toggling skills on and off in the Codex application. Users can search for skills by name, navigate with arrow keys, and press space or enter to enable/disable individual skills, with changes persisted automatically. The view displays a filterable list of skills with their enabled/disabled status and descriptions, handling keyboard input and rendering within the broader bottom pane UI framework.

This module centralizes the logic for filtering and matching built-in slash commands based on sandbox, feature, and permission gates. It provides three helper functions used by both the composer and command popup UI components to determine which commands are visible, find commands by exact name, and check for fuzzy-matched command prefixes—ensuring consistent command availability rules across the interface.

This file provides an interactive configuration UI for customizing the TUI's status bar. It defines all available status line items (model info, git branch, context usage, session details, etc.) and implements a picker view that lets users select which items to display and reorder them, with a live preview of the resulting status line.

# TextArea

This file implements a feature-rich text editor widget for terminal UIs, providing cursor navigation, text editing, and support for atomic "elements" (placeholders or tokens that must be edited as indivisible units). It handles complex Unicode correctly—including grapheme clusters, wide characters, and combining marks—and maintains cursor position invariants (preventing the cursor from landing inside elements). The widget also supports line wrapping with scroll tracking and visual (wrapped) line navigation, making it suitable for multi-line input fields in interactive TUI applications.

This module renders a summary footer for active unified-exec background sessions in the TUI's bottom pane. It maintains a single canonical summary text (e.g., "3 background terminals running · /ps to view · /clean to close") that can be displayed either as a dedicated footer row or embedded inline in the status row, avoiding duplication of formatting logic. The component tracks process count and handles text truncation to fit the available width.

# ChatWidget Summary

`ChatWidget` is the main UI state machine and interaction controller for the Codex TUI chat interface. It owns and orchestrates the conversation transcript, streaming buffers, bottom-pane overlays, and user input handling, while delegating actual agent execution to codex-core.

## Core Responsibilities

**State Management**: Maintains the session transcript (history cells), in-flight streaming content (active cell with revision tracking), token usage, rate limits, collaboration mode, and model/personality settings. Tracks agent-turn lifecycle and MCP startup progress independently to drive the unified "task running" indicator.

**Event Dispatch**: Consumes a stream of protocol events (`EventMsg`) from codex-core and routes them to appropriate handlers—agent deltas feed streaming controllers, exec/MCP events update the active cell, approvals/interrupts are deferred or handled based on write-cycle state, and terminal interactions update the "waiting" UI indicator.

**User Input & Commands**: Converts keyboard events into `Op` submissions and `AppEvent` requests. Handles slash commands (`/model`, `/plan`, `/status`, etc.), manages the double-press quit shortcut (Ctrl+C/Ctrl+D), routes text input through the bottom pane, and orchestrates modal popups (model picker, permissions, review modes, collaboration modes).

**Streaming & Transcription**: Manages two independent stream controllers (for answer and plan content) with adaptive chunking and commit ticks. Tracks when commentary completes to conditionally restore the status row. Defers interrupt-like events (exec begin/end, approvals) during active writes to preserve deterministic FIFO ordering.

**Transcript Overlay Sync**: Exposes `active_cell_transcript_key()` and `active_cell_transcript_lines()` for the `Ctrl+T` overlay to cache and render a live tail of in-flight content without rebuilding on every draw. Revision is bumped on mutations to invalidate the overlay's cache appropriately.

**Status Line & Indicators**: Recomputes footer status-line items (model, branch, token usage, rate limits), manages async git-branch lookup, and synchronizes the bottom-pane running indicator with both agent-turn and MCP-startup lifecycles.

## Key Design Patterns

- **Defer-or-Handle**: Approval requests and exec events are queued during an active stream controller to avoid reordering, then flushed once the stream finishes.
- **Active Cell Grouping**: Consecutive exec calls are coalesced into a single `ExecCell` until a non-exec event forces a flush, reducing visual clutter.
- **Collaboration Masks**: When enabled, user selections (Plan, Default mode) apply a mask over base settings, controlling which model and reasoning effort are used in submissions.
- **Rate Limit Polling**: A background task polls `/usage` every 60 seconds and emits warnings when usage crosses thresholds or triggers a model-nudge prompt.
- **Queued Messages**: While a turn is running or session is configuring, user messages are queued. Once idle, exactly one is auto-submitted to begin the next turn.

This file manages the lifecycle and event forwarding for Codex agent threads in the TUI. It provides three functions to spawn async tasks that bridge the UI and the core Codex engine: `spawn_agent` initializes a new thread and sets up bidirectional communication (accepting operations from the UI and forwarding events back), `spawn_agent_from_existing` does the same for forked or existing threads, and `spawn_op_forwarder` handles operation submission without event subscription. Each function returns a sender channel that allows the UI to submit operations to the Codex backend.

This file implements a queue-based interrupt management system for the chat widget UI. It buffers various protocol events (approvals, user input requests, command execution milestones) that need to be processed by the chat widget, then flushes them in order when ready. This decouples event arrival from UI handling, allowing events to be collected and processed synchronously rather than being handled immediately as they arrive.

This module defines a lightweight header component for chat sessions that displays the active model name. It provides basic state management to track and update which model is currently in use, serving as a simple data holder for the chat UI's session information.

This file manages the skills system in the chat widget UI, enabling users to list, enable/disable, and mention skills in their chat input. It handles the UI flows for skills management (opening menus and popups), tracking skill state changes, and parsing user mentions of skills and applications in text using special syntax like `$skillname` or `[$$skillname](skill://path)`. The file bridges protocol-level skill data with the core domain model and extracts mentions to link them with appropriate skill and app resources.

# Summary

This file contains comprehensive unit and integration tests for the `ChatWidget` component, which is the core TUI interface adapter between Codex protocol events and terminal output. The tests validate event handling, rendering invariants, and user interactions across a wide range of scenarios.

**Key responsibilities:**
- Exercises `ChatWidget`'s event ingestion pipeline (protocol events → display state)
- Validates visual rendering stability via snapshot tests (layout, status indicators, modals)
- Tests user input handling (keyboard, paste, slash commands, approval dialogs)
- Verifies state transitions (task lifecycle, review mode, interrupts, queue management)
- Ensures features like collaboration modes, rate limits, reasoning effort, and plan tools work end-to-end
- Covers edge cases: multi-line commands, nested popups, constrained terminal heights, unified exec processes

The tests treat `ChatWidget` as a black box, injecting synthetic protocol events and inspecting resulting app events, history cells, and rendered output to catch regressions in layout, messaging, and feature behavior.

Defines the command-line interface for the Codex TUI application using `clap`, exposing user-facing arguments like initial prompts, images, model selection, sandbox policies, and approval modes, alongside internal flags for session resumption and forking. This file serves as the single source of truth for all CLI options and their documentation, parsing user input into a structured `Cli` struct that downstream code consumes to configure the agent's behavior.

This module handles clipboard image pasting and path normalization for the TUI. It captures images from the system clipboard (with a WSL fallback on Linux), encodes them to PNG, and provides utilities to parse and normalize filesystem paths from pasted text. The code accounts for platform differences (Android unsupported, special WSL handling) and supports various path formats including file:// URLs, Windows paths, and shell-escaped strings.

This module formats collaboration protocol events into styled terminal UI display cells for the TUI history view. It converts low-level agent spawning, interaction, waiting, and resuming events into human-readable history entries with color-coded status indicators, truncated previews of prompts and responses, and summary statistics of agent states.

This module manages the filtering and selection of collaboration mode presets available in the TUI (terminal user interface). It retrieves collaboration modes from the models manager, filters them to only show TUI-visible modes, and provides helper functions to access specific modes by kind, find defaults, or cycle through the available presets. Essentially, it acts as a bridge between the core collaboration mode configuration and the TUI's need to present only relevant mode options to users.

This module provides color utility functions for the TUI, including brightness detection, alpha blending, and perceptually-accurate color distance calculations. It exists to support color-aware rendering decisions—such as choosing text colors based on background brightness and comparing colors in a way that matches human perception rather than simple RGB distance.

This file implements a custom terminal abstraction layer derived from the Ratatui TUI library. It provides the core rendering infrastructure for drawing widgets to a terminal, managing double-buffered output to minimize flickering and only send terminal updates for cells that have actually changed. The key responsibilities include handling frame rendering via callbacks, computing diffs between consecutive frames to optimize terminal I/O, managing cursor visibility and positioning, and translating high-level drawing commands into low-level terminal escape sequences via the crossterm backend.

This file implements an interactive terminal UI prompt that asks users to choose between their current working directory or a session's recorded working directory when resuming or forking a session. It handles keyboard navigation (arrow keys, vim bindings, number shortcuts), renders a styled modal dialog, and returns the user's selection to the caller.

This file generates formatted debug output displaying the application's configuration state for TUI display. It renders the configuration layer stack (showing which config sources are active and their precedence), all active requirements (sandbox modes, approval policies, network constraints, etc.), and session runtime details like proxy settings. The output is designed to help users and developers understand what configuration is currently in effect and where each setting originates from.

This file renders unified diffs and file changes for display in a terminal UI. It converts file modifications (additions, deletions, updates with unified diffs) into styled terminal lines with line numbers, syntax coloring (green for additions, red for deletions), and intelligent text wrapping that keeps diff markers and line numbers aligned across wrapped lines. The module also handles path display (relativizing to the current working directory when helpful) and generates summary headers showing aggregate change statistics across multiple files.

This module organizes the executable cell UI component by separating data models from rendering logic, exposing key types like `ExecCell` and `CommandOutput` along with rendering utilities for displaying command output, spinners, and tool calls in the terminal interface.

This file defines the data model for representing command execution cells in the TUI, tracking individual command calls with their inputs, outputs, timing, and execution state. It provides methods to manage the lifecycle of executed commands—adding calls, completing them with output, and determining whether a cell represents exploratory (read-only) operations versus user shell commands. The model distinguishes between different execution sources and handles output aggregation and flushing logic for the UI layer.

This file renders executed shell commands and their outputs for display in a terminal UI. It handles formatting command text with syntax highlighting, word wrapping long content to fit screen widths, and intelligently truncating output while preserving both the beginning and end of results. It supports displaying interactive commands (where input was sent to a running process), showing execution status with spinners or checkmarks, and limiting display lines to prevent long outputs from overwhelming the UI.

This module provides utility functions for processing and normalizing shell commands in the TUI layer. It handles escaping command arguments for safe shell execution, stripping shell wrapper invocations (like `bash -lc`) to extract the underlying script, and converting absolute home directory paths to relative paths for display purposes.

This module enables external text editor integration for the TUI, allowing users to compose or edit content in their preferred editor (specified by `VISUAL` or `EDITOR` environment variables). It handles the cross-platform complexity of spawning an editor process, writing seed content to a temporary file, and returning the edited result. The implementation includes special handling for Windows command resolution to properly locate editor shims like `code.cmd`.

This file manages the lifecycle of file search sessions triggered by the `@` token in the chat composer. It listens for user query changes, creates or updates a `codex-file-search` session accordingly, and reports results back to the UI via app events. The manager handles edge cases like empty queries (which drop the session) and directory changes (which reset the current search context).

This file defines animation frame sequences for the TUI, embedding 36-frame animations from text files at compile time across 10 different visual styles (default, codex, openai, blocks, dots, hash, hbars, vbars, shapes, slug). It exists to centralize the loading and exposure of these animation variants so the rendering system can cycle through frames to create visual loading or status animations, with a default timing of 80 milliseconds per frame.

This utility captures the current Git working directory state by retrieving both tracked file changes and untracked files as a unified diff output. It mirrors the behavior of the TypeScript CLI version and handles edge cases like non-Git directories and platform-specific null device paths, running Git operations asynchronously and in parallel where possible.

# Summary

This file defines the `HistoryCell` trait and its implementations, which represent individual displayable units in the Codex TUI conversation history. Each cell type (user messages, agent responses, tool calls, search results, etc.) implements consistent rendering for both the main viewport and the transcript overlay (`Ctrl+T`), with special handling for time-dependent animations via `transcript_animation_tick()` to keep cached overlays in sync during streaming operations.

This file handles inserting formatted text lines into terminal scrollback history while preserving styling and keeping the viewport stable. It manages ANSI escape sequences to manipulate scroll regions, prevent viewport displacement, and apply proper color and text modifiers (bold, italic, etc.) to each character, ensuring that styled content displays consistently whether viewed in the TUI or terminal scrollback.

This module defines keyboard binding abstractions and rendering for the TUI, allowing the application to specify key combinations (like Ctrl+C or Alt+Enter) and display them as human-readable hints in the UI. It handles platform-specific conventions—using the macOS option symbol (⌥) versus "alt" on other systems—and converts key events into stylized text spans for display in the interface.

# Summary

This is the entry point library for Codex's TUI (terminal user interface) application. It orchestrates the entire startup flow: parsing CLI arguments, loading and validating configuration, initializing logging and telemetry, handling authentication and onboarding flows, managing session resume/fork logic, and finally launching the main interactive application. The file essentially bridges CLI invocation and the core TUI app, handling all pre-app initialization concerns like config resolution, trust decisions, login state, and directory-specific settings.

This module implements text wrapping for terminal UI display, breaking input text into visual rows that fit within a specified character width while respecting Unicode character widths (including wide characters like emoji and CJK). The `RowBuilder` tracks both explicit line breaks (from newlines) and implicit hard wraps (from width constraints), and supports dynamic rewrapping when the target width changes—essential for handling terminal resize events in a live TUI environment.

Entry point for the Codex TUI application that parses command-line arguments, merges configuration overrides, and delegates to the main TUI logic while handling token usage reporting. It wraps the inner CLI with a top-level argument parser to allow global config overrides and manages sandbox execution through the arg0 dispatch mechanism.

This module converts markdown text into styled terminal UI lines for display in a TUI interface. It delegates the actual markdown parsing and rendering to a separate renderer, then wraps the results into ratatui Line objects that can be displayed. The tests verify that common markdown constructs—like citations, code blocks, lists, and plain text—render correctly without unwanted reformatting or line splits.

This file renders Markdown content into terminal-compatible styled text for display in a TUI application. It parses Markdown using `pulldown_cmark` and converts it into `ratatui` Line and Span objects with appropriate styling (bold, italic, colors, etc.) for headings, emphasis, code, links, lists, and blockquotes. The renderer also supports word wrapping at a specified width while intelligently preserving indentation for nested lists and blockquotes, and it avoids wrapping code blocks to preserve formatting for copy-paste operations.

This file contains comprehensive unit tests for the markdown rendering functionality in the TUI. It verifies that markdown input (headings, lists, blockquotes, code blocks, inline formatting, etc.) is correctly parsed and converted into styled terminal text for display. The tests cover a wide range of markdown features and edge cases—from simple paragraphs to deeply nested structures, blockquotes with lists, continuation paragraphs in list items, and complex mixed content—ensuring the renderer handles both content accuracy and proper indentation/styling in the terminal UI.

This file implements a streaming markdown renderer that accumulates text deltas and emits fully-rendered markdown lines only when they are logically complete (ending with newlines). It exists to handle incremental markdown rendering from streamed input—like text arriving from an AI model—by buffering partial lines and committing complete ones to avoid flickering or incomplete visual output. The `MarkdownStreamCollector` ensures that markdown syntax (blockquotes, lists, headings, code blocks) is properly styled across chunk boundaries, with comprehensive tests verifying that streamed output matches full-document rendering.

This module encodes and decodes mentions (tool/skill references marked with `$`) in conversation history text, converting between a display format (`$toolname`) and a markdown link format (`[$toolname](path://to/tool)`). It ensures mentions are properly linked to their source paths (app://, mcp://, skill://, or SKILL.md files) while filtering out false positives like environment variables, enabling the UI to track which tools were referenced in conversations.

This file implements a terminal UI prompt that notifies users when an AI model they're using has been deprecated and offers them options to migrate to a newer version. The prompt displays customizable migration messaging (supporting both plain text and markdown formats), and when opt-out is allowed, presents a menu letting users choose between trying the new model or continuing with the existing one. It handles keyboard navigation and can be dismissed via Ctrl+C/Ctrl+D, with results communicated back through a `ModelMigrationOutcome` enum.

This file implements a notification backend that uses the BEL (bell) character—a legacy terminal control code that produces an audible or visual alert—to notify users. The `BelBackend` struct provides a simple notification interface that sends the BEL character (`\x07`) to stdout via crossterm's command execution, working across platforms by preferring ANSI codes even on Windows.

This module provides desktop notification functionality by abstracting over multiple terminal notification backends (OSC 9 and BEL). It auto-detects the best notification method based on the terminal environment, falling back to BEL if OSC 9 isn't supported, and allows users to manually specify their preferred method. The detection logic checks for specific terminal emulators and environment variables to determine OSC 9 compatibility across various shells and launchers.

This file implements OSC 9 (Operating System Command) desktop notifications for the TUI, allowing the application to send system-level notification messages through terminal escape sequences. The `Osc9Backend` provides a simple interface to emit notifications, while the `PostNotification` command translates those messages into ANSI escape codes that compatible terminals can interpret as desktop notifications.

This file implements the authentication UI flow for the Codex TUI, allowing users to sign in via ChatGPT, device code, or API key. The `AuthModeWidget` manages the state machine of different sign-in screens (mode selection, browser continuation, success messages, etc.), handles keyboard input and text entry, and renders appropriate prompts and instructions for each authentication method. It also enforces workspace-level restrictions on which login methods are allowed and integrates with the underlying auth manager to persist credentials.

This file implements headless ChatGPT authentication using device codes, allowing users to sign in without opening a browser directly within the TUI. It orchestrates the device code login flow—requesting a one-time code, displaying it to the user with a verification URL, and polling for completion—while falling back to browser-based login if device codes aren't available. The rendering function formats the device code and instructions as terminal UI elements with animations, and helper functions manage state transitions to prevent race conditions during async login operations.

This module organizes the onboarding flow for the TUI application by aggregating sub-modules for authentication, welcome screens, and trust directory selection. It serves as the entry point for new user setup and configuration, exposing key components like `TrustDirectorySelection` while keeping internal implementation details private.

This file implements the main onboarding flow UI for the Codex application, orchestrating a sequence of screens (welcome, authentication, and directory trust) that users complete on first setup. It manages keyboard input, rendering, and state transitions across these steps, ultimately returning the user's authentication and directory trust decisions once the flow completes.

This file implements an onboarding UI screen that asks users whether they trust the current directory before Codex proceeds. When users select "Yes", it marks the directory (or its git root) as trusted in the config; when they select "No", it quits the onboarding flow. The widget handles keyboard navigation and renders a terminal UI with options and error messaging.

The `WelcomeWidget` renders the welcome screen for Codex's onboarding flow, displaying an animated ASCII art background along with a welcome message. It handles keyboard input (Ctrl+.) to cycle through animation variants and intelligently hides the animation when the terminal viewport is too small to display it properly. The widget's visibility in the onboarding flow depends on the user's login status.

This file implements an interactive terminal UI for selecting which local open-source LLM provider (LM Studio or Ollama) to use. It checks the status of each provider by probing their default ports, auto-selects if only one is running, and displays a keyboard-navigable selection widget if manual choice is needed. The selected provider is then persisted as the user's default preference.

This file implements overlay UI panels for the TUI, specifically pager-style views like the transcript history overlay (`Ctrl+T`). The transcript overlay displays committed conversation history plus an optional "live tail" from the currently streaming active cell, with intelligent caching that recomputes the tail only when terminal width, cell mutations, or animation state changes. It provides vim-like scrolling navigation (arrows, page-up/down, home/end) and maintains scroll position intelligently—keeping the view pinned to the bottom when new content arrives unless the user has manually scrolled up. A generic `PagerView` widget handles the scrolling mechanics and rendering infrastructure for both transcript and static overlay types.

This file provides a public, simplified wrapper around the internal `ChatComposer` widget, exposing multi-line text input functionality for reuse across other crates like codex-cloud-tasks. It abstracts away the complexity of the composer's internal event system and paste-handling logic, offering a clean API with key submission detection, custom hint overrides, and rendering support while maintaining the underlying input behaviors like Shift+Enter for newlines and intelligent paste detection.
This module serves as the public API for reusable UI widgets, currently exporting the composer input component that handles text composition functionality for the TUI interface.

This file implements syntax highlighting for Bash scripts in the TUI by converting raw script text into styled line segments. It uses tree-sitter's parsing and highlight query system to identify language constructs (keywords, strings, operators, etc.) and applies visual styling rules—dimming comments, strings, and operators while keeping other elements normal. The module streams highlighting events to preserve style boundaries across line breaks, with fallback to unstyled output if parsing fails.

This utility module provides helpers for manipulating ratatui `Line` and `Span` objects used in the TUI rendering pipeline. It converts borrowed lines into owned static lifetimes (necessary for storing UI elements), detects blank lines, and prefixes lines with different styling for the first versus subsequent lines—enabling features like indentation or line numbering in rendered text output.

This module provides rendering utilities and layout helpers for the TUI. It defines an `Insets` struct for managing spacing (padding) around rectangular areas and extends ratatui's `Rect` type with an `inset` method to easily shrink layout regions by specified margins—commonly needed when rendering UI elements with padding or borders.

This file defines a trait-based abstraction for composable UI rendering in a terminal UI system. The `Renderable` trait provides a contract for any drawable element to implement `render()`, `desired_height()`, and optional `cursor_pos()` methods, enabling flexible layout composition. It includes implementations for common types (strings, paragraphs, options, arcs) and several layout container types—`ColumnRenderable` for vertical stacking, `FlexRenderable` for flex-based space allocation (similar to Flutter's Flex widget), `RowRenderable` for horizontal layouts, and `InsetRenderable` for adding padding—allowing nested, declarative UI construction without tightly coupling rendering logic to specific widget types.

This file implements an interactive terminal UI for browsing and selecting previous Codex sessions to resume or fork. It provides cursor-based pagination, real-time search filtering, and a sortable table view displaying session metadata (creation/update timestamps, git branch, working directory, and conversation previews). The picker handles background page loading asynchronously, deduplicates results across paginated responses, and dynamically shows/hides columns based on terminal width to keep the conversation preview readable.

This file provides UI components for rendering individual rows in a selection list within the TUI. It formats each option with a numbered prefix (showing a visual indicator `›` when selected), applies appropriate styling (cyan for selected, dimmed for inactive), and handles text wrapping to fit the available width. The two functions allow creation of selection rows with or without a dimmed appearance.

This file implements session logging for the Codex TUI, recording all inbound application events and outbound protocol operations to a JSONL file for debugging and analysis purposes. It provides a lazy-initialized singleton logger that writes timestamped, structured records whenever enabled via environment variables, capturing context like working directory, model configuration, and detailed event/operation payloads. The logging is optional and gracefully degrades if I/O errors occur, making it useful for understanding user interactions and system behavior without impacting core functionality.

This file implements a shimmer animation effect for terminal text that sweeps across characters over time. It uses a cosine-based gradient to create a smooth "highlight" band that travels continuously from left to right, adapting to the terminal's color capabilities by using true RGB colors when available or falling back to bold/dim modifiers for basic terminals. The animation is synchronized to process start time to ensure consistent timing across application restarts.

This file provides utility functions for displaying and filtering skills in the TUI. It handles extracting display names and descriptions from skill metadata (with fallbacks to default values), truncating skill names for UI layout, and fuzzy-matching skills against user filter input to enable searchable skill lists.

This file defines the `/` slash command system for the Codex TUI, enumerating all available commands (like `/model`, `/review`, `/new`) and their metadata. It serves as the central registry for command definitions, including user-visible descriptions, support for inline arguments, and availability constraints (e.g., which commands can run while a task is in progress). The enum ordering deliberately reflects presentation priority in the command popup rather than alphabetical order.

Defines an enumeration that represents the different ways account status information can be displayed in the TUI, distinguishing between ChatGPT accounts (which show email and plan details) and API key authentication. This enables the status display component to render appropriate account information based on the authentication method being used.

This file implements the visual display of the `/status` command for the Codex TUI. It constructs and renders a formatted status card showing current session details including the active model, working directory, permissions, token usage metrics, rate limits, account information, and collaboration session metadata. The card adapts its content and layout based on available terminal width and dynamically includes/excludes fields based on what data is available.

This file provides formatting utilities for rendering structured status information in a terminal UI. It handles the layout and alignment of label-value pairs (like "Status: ready" or "Memory: 512MB"), calculating proper spacing and indentation to create a clean columnar display. It also includes helpers for truncating multi-line text output to fit within available terminal width while preserving styling.

This file provides formatting and display helper functions for the status UI in Codex. It handles converting various data types (models, agents, account info, tokens, paths, timestamps) into human-readable strings suitable for terminal display, including logic like path relativization, token count abbreviation (K/M/B/T), and intelligent truncation based on available width.

This module transforms protocol-level data snapshots into display-ready structures for the TUI's status output, status lines, and footer information. It acts as an adapter layer between raw transport data and rendering logic, with `rate_limits` as the primary integration point for converting rate-limit window snapshots into user-friendly local-time labels and availability classifications.

This module transforms API rate-limit and credit snapshots from the protocol layer into display-ready data structures for the TUI's status views. It handles timestamp-relative formatting (reset times, staleness detection), multi-window rate-limit composition with smart labeling, and credit balance rendering, while providing helpers for progress bar visualization. The key responsibility is centralizing rate-limit presentation logic so status displays remain consistent whether shown in the `/status` panel or status line.

This file contains unit tests for the status display output functionality in the TUI. It validates how various system states—including token usage, rate limits, credits, permissions, and session metadata—are rendered in the terminal status card. The tests use snapshot assertions and helper functions to ensure the status display correctly formats information across different configurations, terminal widths, and data scenarios (such as unlimited credits, zero balance, stale rate limits, and reasoning token details).

This widget displays a live status indicator row that appears above the composer while an agent task is running. It manages an animated spinner, elapsed time tracking, an optional interrupt hint (with ESC key guidance), and contextual inline messages—all rendered on a single line to avoid layout shifts. The widget also handles optional wrapped details text below the header and includes timer pause/resume functionality to track elapsed time accurately across different task states.

This file implements an adaptive chunking policy that controls how quickly streamed content is displayed in the terminal UI during commit animations. It operates as a two-mode system: "Smooth" mode drains one line per baseline tick to maintain steady pacing, while "CatchUp" mode drains all queued lines when backlog pressure rises (measured by queue depth or age). The policy uses hysteresis thresholds and hold windows to prevent rapid mode-switching near decision boundaries, ensuring the display remains responsive to bursts without flickering between modes. This source-agnostic approach depends only on queue depth and line age, not on stream identity or explicit throughput targets.

This module orchestrates the draining of queued stream data during animation ticks, bridging a chunking policy (which decides *how much* to drain) with concrete stream controllers (which perform the actual draining). It captures queue pressure across controllers, consults the adaptive chunking policy for a drain decision, applies that decision to both stream types, and returns the resulting history cells—all while preserving order and leaving UI mutations to the caller.

This file implements two controller classes that manage real-time streaming of text content into a terminal UI with animated line-by-line display. `StreamController` handles general agent message streaming by buffering incoming text deltas, committing complete lines when newlines arrive, and emitting styled message blocks with optional headers, while `PlanStreamController` specializes in streaming proposed plan content with markdown styling and padding. Both controllers support flexible animation pacing through single-line and batch-line commit methods, allowing the UI to display streamed output incrementally rather than all at once.

This module provides the core streaming queue abstraction for the TUI's transcript rendering pipeline. `StreamState` manages an ordered FIFO queue of rendered markdown lines along with their enqueue timestamps, allowing higher-level modules (`controller`, `chunking`, `commit_tick`) to apply adaptive draining policies based on queue pressure and age without needing to inspect the actual text content. The design enforces strict queue ordering so that policy decisions can be made consistently across message and plan streams.

This file defines the visual styling for different UI elements in the terminal interface, specifically adapting colors based on the detected terminal background. It provides functions that generate appropriate background colors for user messages and proposed plans by blending semi-transparent overlays with the terminal's background, ensuring text remains readable on both light and dark terminals.

This file manages terminal color palette detection and conversion, enabling the TUI to display colors that match the terminal's capabilities. It queries the terminal's foreground and background colors on Unix systems, provides a function to find the closest displayable color to a target RGB value (using perceptual distance), and maintains a version counter so that cached UI renderings can be invalidated when the terminal's color scheme changes. The large `XTERM_COLORS` lookup table defines the standard 256-color palette, with indices 0-15 being theme-dependent and indices 16-255 being consistent across most terminals.

This file provides a testable terminal backend by wrapping ratatui's CrosstermBackend with a vt100 parser, enabling unit tests to simulate terminal behavior without actual stdout I/O. It avoids problematic crossterm calls like querying real terminal size and instead uses the mocked vt100 parser's state, while remaining compatible with ratatui's Backend trait so tests can exercise UI rendering logic in isolation.

This file provides text formatting utilities for the TUI, with a focus on intelligently displaying content within terminal constraints. It handles truncating text while respecting Unicode grapheme boundaries, compacting JSON for better line wrapping in the terminal UI, shortening file paths with strategic ellipsis placement, and joining strings with proper English punctuation. The functions are designed to work around limitations in the Ratatui library's text wrapping capabilities while preserving readability and handling multi-byte Unicode characters correctly.

This file manages contextual tooltips and announcements displayed to Codex CLI users at startup. It selects from a pool of hardcoded tips or remote announcements based on the user's plan type (Free, Plus, Enterprise, etc.), platform (macOS vs. others), and feature flags, with a small chance of showing a random tip instead of promotional content. The announcement submodule fetches and parses time-sensitive, version-aware announcements from a remote TOML file, applying date ranges and regex filters to determine eligibility.

This file implements the core terminal UI initialization and state management for the Codex application. It handles setting up and restoring terminal modes (raw mode, keyboard enhancement, alternate screen), managing the lifecycle of crossterm event handling, and providing high-level operations like pausing events to run external programs or entering alternate screen mode. The `Tui` struct serves as the main interface for the rest of the application to interact with the terminal, including drawing frames, managing viewport state, and sending desktop notifications when the terminal loses focus.

This file implements event stream management for the TUI, enabling the application to pause and resume terminal input handling without leaving stdin in a broken state. The `EventBroker` manages a shared crossterm event source that can be dropped and recreated on demand, while `TuiEventStream` multiplexes keyboard/terminal events with draw signals and exposes them as a single async stream. This architecture solves a critical problem: crossterm's EventStream continues reading stdin even when not actively polled, which can steal input from other processes (like spawned editors) or capture responses meant for them; by fully dropping the stream during pause, the TUI completely relinquishes stdin access until it resumes.

Provides a lightweight frame-rate limiter that caps draw notifications at 120 FPS by clamping requested frame deadlines forward if they would violate the minimum inter-frame interval. This prevents widgets from triggering excessive redraws faster than human perception allows, reducing unnecessary work in the async frame scheduler.

This file implements a frame scheduling system that batches redraw requests from various parts of the TUI into efficient, coalesced draw operations. The `FrameRequester` serves as a cloneable handle that widgets and background tasks can use to request immediate or delayed frame redraws, while an internal `FrameScheduler` actor collects multiple requests and coalesces them into single notifications sent to the main event loop, ensuring smooth animations without unnecessary redrawing and respecting a 120 FPS rate limit.

This file manages terminal suspend and resume behavior for the TUI when the user presses Ctrl+Z. It captures whether the alternate screen is active at suspend time, preserves the cursor position, and then coordinates the proper viewport restoration when the process resumes—either by realigning the inline viewport or re-entering the alternate screen UI. The `SuspendContext` type uses shared atomic state so suspend/resume logic can work across async tasks without holding references to the main TUI.

Defines shared UI constants that govern horizontal spacing and alignment across the TUI, specifically reserving two columns for left-side gutters and prefixes used by interactive elements like the chat composer and status indicators. This ensures consistent visual alignment when text wraps or multiple UI components are displayed side-by-side.

Defines the update mechanism for the Codex CLI by identifying which package manager installed it (npm, bun, or Homebrew) and providing the appropriate command-line arguments to perform an upgrade. The detection logic checks environment variables and executable path locations to determine the installation method, enabling the TUI to trigger the correct update command when exiting.

This file implements an interactive modal dialog that prompts users to update Codex when a newer version is available. It manages the prompt's lifecycle—checking if an update is needed, rendering the UI with three options (Update Now, Skip, or Skip Until Next Version), handling keyboard navigation and selection, and returning the user's choice to either proceed with the update or continue without updating.

This module manages update checking and notifications for the Codex CLI. It periodically fetches the latest available version from GitHub releases (or Homebrew's API if installed via Homebrew), caches the result locally, and determines whether to show an upgrade banner to the user. It also handles user dismissals so the same version upgrade prompt isn't shown repeatedly.
Provides a compile-time constant that embeds the Codex CLI version number from the package manifest, allowing the application to report its version to users without runtime overhead.

This module provides text wrapping functionality for terminal UI (TUI) rendering, breaking styled text lines into display-width-constrained segments while preserving formatting and supporting customizable indentation. It wraps the `textwrap` crate with ratatui-specific types to handle visual line breaks that account for Unicode display width, styled spans, and optional initial/subsequent line indentation (useful for features like bullet points or nested lists).

This file serves as the entry point for the codebase's integration test suite, aggregating test modules from the `tests/suite/` directory into a single test binary. It conditionally includes backend tests when the `vt100-tests` feature is enabled and maintains a reference to the `codex_cli` dependency to ensure it isn't incorrectly removed by dependency-stripping tools.

Aggregates integration test modules for the TUI component into a single test suite, organizing former standalone tests for startup behavior, status indicators, terminal history, and live commit functionality under a unified namespace.

This is a regression test that verifies the Codex CLI handles malformed configuration gracefully without panicking during startup. It creates a test environment with an invalid rules file (a file instead of a directory), launches the CLI, and asserts that it exits with an error message rather than crashing. The test also includes PTY (pseudo-terminal) handling to simulate terminal interactions needed for the TUI to initialize properly.

This is a regression test that verifies the `StatusIndicatorWidget` properly sanitizes ANSI escape sequences so they don't leak raw escape bytes into the rendering buffer. The test validates the public contract of the `ansi_escape_line()` function, ensuring it strips ANSI color codes (like `\x1b[31m` for red) while preserving the actual text content.

This test suite validates the history insertion functionality for a terminal UI, ensuring that text content (including multi-byte characters, ANSI styling, and long lines) is correctly rendered and wrapped within a constrained viewport. The tests verify correct handling of edge cases like emoji/CJK characters, word wrapping behavior, line wrapping of oversized tokens, and cursor positioning after insertion.

This test validates that the live text wrapping system correctly commits rows to terminal history when they overflow a configurable buffer size. It verifies that when a `RowBuilder` accumulates more rows than a threshold, the excess rows are properly flushed to the terminal's history while remaining rows stay in the live buffer for further editing.

This file re-exports the `VT100Backend` test implementation from the main source directory, making it available as a public module for integration tests. It serves as a bridge to allow the test backend defined in the core codebase to be used across the test suite without duplication.

This file defines `AbsolutePathBuf`, a type-safe wrapper around `PathBuf` that guarantees paths are absolute and normalized. It exists to safely handle path resolution in configuration or serialization contexts where relative paths need to be converted to absolute ones using a configurable base path, with support for home directory expansion on Unix systems. The `AbsolutePathBufGuard` provides thread-local context during deserialization to supply the base path needed for resolving relative paths from JSON or other serialized formats.

This module defines reusable approval and sandbox policy presets for the Codex system. It provides a `ApprovalPreset` struct that pairs an approval policy (controlling when user confirmation is needed) with a sandbox policy (controlling what system resources are accessible), along with UI-agnostic metadata like labels and descriptions. The `builtin_approval_presets()` function exposes three predefined presets—read-only, default, and full-access—designed to be consumed by both TUI and MCP server interfaces without duplicating policy configuration logic.

This file provides a thread-safe LRU cache wrapper (`BlockingLruCache`) designed for async Tokio environments that gracefully degrades to a no-op cache when called outside a runtime. It also exports a SHA-1 hashing utility for generating content-based cache keys. The cache is useful for memoizing expensive computations in async code without blocking the runtime, while providing a safe fallback for non-async contexts.

This library provides cross-build-system abstractions for locating binaries and resources in tests. It enables code to transparently work with both Cargo and Bazel test environments by resolving paths differently depending on which build system is running: Cargo uses direct filesystem paths while Bazel uses a runfiles manifest system. The main utilities include `cargo_bin()` to locate compiled binaries, `find_resource!()` to locate test data files, and `repo_root()` to find the repository root, all handling the path resolution differences automatically.

Defines the `--approval-mode` CLI argument that controls when user approval is required for command execution, supporting four modes (untrusted, on-failure, on-request, never) and converting them to the core protocol's `AskForApproval` type for downstream handling.

This module enables CLI users to override configuration values using `-c key=value` flags across Codex tools. It parses these overrides into key/value pairs, intelligently converts values from TOML syntax (or treats them as literal strings), and applies them to a configuration tree by creating intermediate nested objects as needed. The design allows flexible configuration without requiring users to know the exact syntax—for example, `-c model=o3` works without quotes while `-c sandbox_permissions=["disk-full-read-access"]` correctly parses complex types.

This utility formats environment variables for display while masking their values for security purposes. It takes a map of environment variables and a list of variable names, then outputs them in a sorted, human-readable format with all values redacted as asterisks—useful for logging or debugging without exposing sensitive information like API keys or tokens.

This module serves as the public interface for CLI utilities and argument handling, re-exporting core components for command-line configuration and mode selection across the codebase. It organizes support for approval mode, sandbox mode, environment formatting, and configuration overrides that can be applied via CLI arguments.

Defines a CLI argument type for the `--sandbox` flag that allows users to specify execution sandbox policy (read-only, workspace-write, or full access) without exposing complex configuration details on the command line. It provides a simplified enum that maps to the underlying `SandboxMode` protocol type, with advanced customization still available through config file overrides.

This utility provides functions to convert elapsed time durations into human-readable, compact string formats suitable for displaying in logs or user-facing output. It handles three ranges: sub-second durations display as milliseconds, durations under a minute show decimal seconds, and longer durations display as minutes and seconds (e.g., "1m 15s").

Provides case-insensitive fuzzy substring matching for UI filtering with proper Unicode handling. The function returns matched character positions in the original string and a score that penalizes scattered matches while rewarding prefix matches, enabling features like search-as-you-type with visual highlighting of matched characters.

This file provides a wrapper around the system `git apply` command that applies unified diffs to a repository and parses the results into structured output. The main entry point, `apply_git_patch`, writes a diff to a temporary file, shells out to git with appropriate flags, and extracts applied/skipped/conflicted paths from the command's output. It supports dry-run validation via a preflight mode (`--check`) and revert operations (`-R`), making it useful for code generation or patch-preview scenarios where callers need to safely test or inspect what changes would occur before committing them. The file also includes a sophisticated regex-based parser (ported from VS Code) that handles git's varied output messages across different error and success conditions.

This file provides utilities for finding the merge base between the current HEAD and a specified branch in a Git repository. It intelligently handles cases where a branch has an upstream remote version that's ahead of the local version, preferring to compute the merge base against the remote branch in those scenarios. The module gracefully handles missing branches or repositories without a HEAD yet by returning `None` rather than errors.

Defines the error types for git worktree management operations in the Codex tooling. It captures various failure modes including git command execution failures, UTF-8 decoding issues, invalid repository paths, and filesystem traversal errors, providing structured error reporting for upstream handling.

This file implements a "ghost commit" feature for Git repositories—a mechanism to create automatic snapshots of the working tree state and restore to previous snapshots. Ghost commits capture tracked file changes, untracked files, and deletions as detached commits without affecting the user's branch history, enabling an undo-like functionality. The implementation provides configurable thresholds to exclude large files and directories (like `node_modules` or build artifacts) from snapshots while preserving them during restoration, and tracks which untracked files existed before the snapshot to avoid deleting newly created files during restore operations.

This is the public API module for a Git utilities library that provides high-level operations for managing repository state, particularly around "ghost commits"—temporary snapshot commits that capture the full state of a repository including untracked files. It exports functions for applying git patches, managing branches, creating/restoring ghost commits, and handling platform-specific operations, while defining the `GhostCommit` data structure that represents these snapshots with metadata about pre-existing untracked files and directories.

This module provides low-level git operations for the Codex tooling, wrapping subprocess calls to the `git` command with error handling and path validation. It includes utilities to verify repositories exist, resolve HEAD commits and repository roots, normalize relative paths with bounds checking, and execute arbitrary git commands while capturing and validating their output. The functions serve as building blocks for higher-level git interactions that need reliable, context-rich error reporting.

This file provides a platform-abstracted interface for creating symbolic links, with separate implementations for Unix and Windows systems. On Unix, it delegates directly to the native symlink function, while on Windows it inspects the source file type to determine whether to create a directory or file symlink, since Windows requires the target type to be specified upfront. It explicitly prevents compilation on unsupported platforms.

This utility locates the Codex configuration directory by checking the `CODEX_HOME` environment variable first, falling back to `~/.codex` if unset. When `CODEX_HOME` is explicitly set, it validates that the path exists and is a directory before canonicalizing it; when unset, it returns the default path without validation. The module exists to provide a single, testable entry point for configuration directory resolution across the Codex application.

Defines the error types for image processing operations, including failures during reading, decoding, and encoding images. Provides contextual information like file paths and image formats to help diagnose which step failed, and includes a helper method to distinguish decoding errors from other I/O issues.

This module handles image loading, resizing, and encoding for upload to the Codex API. It reads image files from disk, constrains them to a maximum of 2048×768 pixels (resizing with triangle filtering if needed), and encodes them as PNG or JPEG with appropriate MIME types. The module uses an LRU cache keyed on file content hash to avoid reprocessing identical images, and includes smart async-aware file I/O that adapts based on whether it's running inside a Tokio runtime.

This library provides a conversion function that transforms JSON values into equivalent TOML values, handling all JSON types including nested objects and arrays. It exists to enable interoperability between JSON and TOML configuration formats, treating JSON nulls as empty strings since TOML lacks a native null type.

This module provides shared utilities for managing open-source LLM providers (LM Studio and Ollama) across the TUI and execution layers. It exposes functions to retrieve default models for each provider and to verify that a provider is properly configured and ready to use, abstracting away provider-specific setup logic behind a common interface.

This module provides a unified interface for spawning and managing processes across platforms, supporting both interactive PTY (pseudo-terminal) and non-interactive pipe-based execution modes. It exports spawn helpers, process handles, and platform detection utilities while maintaining backwards-compatible type aliases for legacy code. The module abstracts away platform-specific complexity (particularly Windows ConPTY support) behind a common API.

This module spawns and manages subprocesses using standard pipes (rather than PTYs), providing async handles for reading output and writing input. It abstracts away platform-specific process termination logic (Unix process groups vs. Windows API) and multiplexes stdout/stderr through broadcast channels so multiple consumers can receive output simultaneously. The file exists to offer a lightweight alternative to PTY-based process spawning when terminal emulation isn't needed.

This file defines the core handle types for managing interactive child processes in a PTY (pseudo-terminal) or pipe environment. `ProcessHandle` wraps all the necessary channels, task handles, and process state to allow callers to write to stdin, read stdout/stderr, check exit status, and terminate a running process, while `SpawnedProcess` bundles everything needed to interact with a newly spawned child process.

This module provides OS-specific utilities for managing spawned child processes and their entire process groups, ensuring they can be reliably created in isolation and cleaned up completely. It abstracts Unix-specific operations like creating new process groups, detaching from the controlling terminal, setting up parent-death signals (Linux), and killing entire process hierarchies—with no-op implementations for non-Unix platforms.

This file implements cross-platform PTY (pseudo-terminal) process spawning for the Codex system. It abstracts away platform differences (Windows ConPTY vs Unix PTY) to spawn shell processes with attached stdin/stdout and provide async handles for reading output, writing input, and monitoring exit status. The spawned process runs in a separate thread while tokio tasks manage bidirectional communication with the PTY master.

This file contains integration tests for the PTY (pseudo-terminal) and pipe process spawning functionality. The tests verify that both spawning mechanisms correctly handle process lifecycle management, I/O handling, and platform-specific behaviors (Windows vs Unix), ensuring the library can reliably launch and communicate with interactive subprocesses like Python REPLs and shell commands.

This file implements Windows ConPty (Pseudo-Console) support for PTY operations by wrapping the lower-level PsuedoCon abstraction. It provides master and slave PTY types that handle terminal resizing, command spawning, and I/O redirection on Windows systems, adapting the portable_pty interface to work with Windows' ConPty API. The implementation was adapted from the WezTerm project under MIT license.

This file provides Windows-specific child process management for PTY operations, implementing wrappers around Windows process handles to check exit status, terminate processes, and await completion. It adapts the `portable_pty` library's traits (`Child` and `ChildKiller`) to work with Windows process APIs, including support for async/await patterns through Rust futures. The code is adapted from the WezTerm terminal emulator project.

This file provides a safe Rust wrapper around Windows process thread attributes, specifically for attaching a pseudo-console (PTY) to a spawned process. It encapsulates the low-level Windows API calls (`InitializeProcThreadAttributeList`, `UpdateProcThreadAttribute`, `DeleteProcThreadAttributeList`) into a managed struct that handles memory lifecycle and provides a convenient `set_pty()` method to configure the pseudo-console before process creation.

This file provides Windows pseudo-console (ConPTY) support for spawning and managing child processes with proper terminal emulation. It wraps Windows' native ConPTY APIs (CreatePseudoConsole, ResizePseudoConsole, ClosePseudoConsole) to create virtual terminal environments, and handles the low-level details of process creation including command-line argument quoting, environment variable setup, and working directory resolution. The code was originally sourced from the WezTerm project and is adapted to work with this codebase's PTY abstraction layer.

This module implements a token-based readiness flag for coordinating async initialization in Tokio applications. It allows multiple async tasks to subscribe for authorization tokens, then marks the system ready only when an authorized token holder explicitly signals completion—enabling fine-grained control over when dependent operations can proceed. The flag uses atomic operations for fast reads and watch channels to efficiently wake waiting tasks.

This module provides a one-time initialization function that installs a process-wide rustls cryptographic provider using the `ring` backend. It exists to handle the ambiguity that arises when both `ring` and `aws-lc-rs` crypto providers are available in the dependency graph, ensuring rustls has a deterministic default provider rather than failing at runtime.

This file generates a human-readable summary of the active configuration settings for the Codex sandbox environment. It extracts key operational parameters like working directory, model selection, approval policies, and sandbox restrictions into a list of key-value pairs that can be displayed to users or logged for debugging purposes.

This module serves as a public interface for sandbox analysis utilities, exposing two main functions: one for generating summary entries from sandbox configurations and another for summarizing sandbox security policies. It acts as an organizational layer that aggregates related sandbox documentation and policy analysis tools into a single, cohesive API.

This utility converts sandbox policy configurations into human-readable summary strings for display purposes. It handles multiple sandbox types (full access, read-only, external, and workspace-write) and includes relevant details like network access status and writable directory paths in the formatted output.

This utility module provides string manipulation helpers for handling byte-constrained truncation and metric tag sanitization. It offers functions to safely trim strings at UTF-8 character boundaries (either from the start or end) while respecting a byte budget, and sanitizes metric tag values by replacing invalid characters with underscores and enforcing a 256-character limit, with fallback to "unspecified" for empty or non-alphanumeric-only values.

This build script embeds a Windows manifest file into the compiled binary for the Windows Sandbox setup executable. The manifest specifies Windows-specific metadata and configuration requirements (such as admin privileges or compatibility settings) that the OS needs when launching the application.

This file provides Windows Access Control List (ACL) utilities for managing file and directory permissions in the sandbox environment. It wraps low-level Windows security APIs to enable querying, checking, and modifying ACEs (Access Control Entries) for specific SIDs (Security Identifiers) on filesystem paths and kernel objects. The module supports operations like verifying if a SID has read/write permissions, adding or revoking access rules, and configuring special permissions like write denial—capabilities essential for isolating and controlling process access within the sandbox.

This file computes the filesystem access policy for the Windows sandbox by determining which paths should be allowed or denied based on the sandbox configuration. It handles writable workspace roots, respects optional temporary directory restrictions, and automatically denies access to `.git` directories within writable areas to prevent accidental repository modifications in sandboxed environments.

This file audits the filesystem for world-writable directories that could pose a sandbox security risk, then applies access control restrictions to protect them. It performs a time-bounded scan starting with the current working directory and common system paths, identifies locations where "everyone" has write access, and applies capability-based deny ACEs to prevent sandboxed processes from writing to those locations (unless they're in the workspace's writable roots).

This is a platform-specific entry point that conditionally routes execution to Windows-specific command runner logic or fails gracefully on non-Windows platforms. It exists to ensure the command runner binary only runs on Windows while delegating the actual implementation to a separate Windows-specific module.

This is the platform-specific entry point for a Windows sandbox setup utility. It conditionally compiles to either delegate to Windows-specific initialization logic or panic with a clear error message on non-Windows platforms, ensuring the tool only runs where it's designed to function.

This file manages capability SIDs (Security Identifiers) for Windows sandbox isolation, generating and persisting unique identifiers that control access permissions across different workspaces and working directories. It handles loading existing SIDs from disk, creating new ones when needed, and ensuring that equivalent paths (different spellings/casings) map to the same workspace SID to maintain consistent access control policies.

This is the Windows sandbox command runner—a restricted subprocess launcher that executes commands within a sandboxed environment with limited capabilities. It reads a JSON request specifying the command, working directory, environment, and security policy; creates a restricted access token based on the policy (read-only or workspace-write); opens named pipes for stdio; and spawns the process using `CreateProcessAsUserW` with the constrained token, optionally enforcing a timeout and job termination. The file exists to isolate untrusted code execution on Windows while preserving access only to explicitly granted capabilities.

This module creates Windows directory junctions (symbolic links) to map requested working directories into a sandbox-managed location. It hashes the target path to generate a unique junction name, stores junctions under `%USERPROFILE%/.codex/.sandbox/cwd/`, and reuses existing junctions when possible to avoid repeatedly shelling out to the `mklink` command, making the sandbox's hot path more efficient.

This module provides Windows DPAPI (Data Protection API) encryption and decryption functionality, wrapping the native `CryptProtectData` and `CryptUnprotectData` Windows APIs to enable secure data encryption at the machine level. It exists to allow different privilege levels (elevated and non-elevated processes) within a Windows sandbox environment to encrypt and decrypt sensitive data using the same machine-scoped credentials, abstracting away the complexity of direct Windows API calls and memory management.

This file implements the core Windows sandbox execution layer, launching a command runner process under a restricted sandbox user account while applying security policies and capturing its output. It handles process creation via `CreateProcessWithLogonW`, sets up inter-process communication through named pipes, manages security descriptors and capability SIDs for access control, and coordinates stdout/stderr capture with timeout support. A non-Windows stub ensures the code compiles on other platforms while rejecting sandbox requests at runtime.

This file manages environment variable setup for Windows sandbox execution, particularly for isolating and restricting process behavior.
